Bombay HC Protects Litigant's Access to Justice: Halts Singapore Court Order Against Shaadi.com CEO Anupam Mittal

blocks.html: In a significant legal development, the Bombay High Court has intervened to safeguard the access to justice for Anupam Mittal, the CEO of Shaadi.com. The court temporarily suspended the enforcement of an anti-suit permanent injunction order issued by the Singapore High Court, which had barred Mittal from pursuing his petition before the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT). This NCLT petition alleged the oppression and mismanagement of People Interactive (India) Pvt. Ltd., the parent company of Shaadi.com. Justice Manish Pitale, in his ruling, emphasized the importance of granting litigants their right to access justice.
blocks.text: In a significant legal development, the Bombay High Court has intervened to safeguard the access to justice for Anupam Mittal, the CEO of Shaadi.com. The court temporarily suspended the enforcement of an anti-suit permanent injunction order issued by the Singapore High Court, which had barred Mittal from pursuing his petition before the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT). This NCLT petition alleged the oppression and mismanagement of People Interactive (India) Pvt. Ltd., the parent company of Shaadi.com. Justice Manish Pitale, in his ruling, emphasized the importance of granting litigants their right to access justice.
Court Upholds Litigant's Right to Pursue Legal Remedy

blocks.html: Justice Manish Pitale underscored that disputes related to oppression and mismanagement are non-arbitrable in India. He rejected the idea that the principle of comity of courts should prevent a litigant from pursuing their only available legal remedy. In his words, "The principle of comity of Courts is well recognized, but the said principle cannot override the aforesaid valuable right of a litigant to access of justice, particularly when an injunction, as in this case, an anti-suit injunction, is issued by a foreign Court having the effect of interference with or preventing the plaintiff from pursuing the only legal remedy available in the facts and circumstances of the case."
blocks.text: Justice Manish Pitale underscored that disputes related to oppression and mismanagement are non-arbitrable in India. He rejected the idea that the principle of comity of courts should prevent a litigant from pursuing their only available legal remedy. In his words, "The principle of comity of Courts is well recognized, but the said principle cannot override the aforesaid valuable right of a litigant to access of justice, particularly when an injunction, as in this case, an anti-suit injunction, is issued by a foreign Court having the effect of interference with or preventing the plaintiff from pursuing the only legal remedy available in the facts and circumstances of the case."
The Background of the Legal Battle

blocks.html: The legal dispute in question revolves around a Shareholders Agreement (SHA) executed in February 2006. Anupam Mittal, along with his cousins Anand and Navin Mittal, entered into this agreement with People Interactive and Westbridge Ventures II Investment Holdings, a private equity fund incorporated in Mauritius. Through the SHA, Westbridge Ventures acquired a significant share of People Interactive.
blocks.text: The legal dispute in question revolves around a Shareholders Agreement (SHA) executed in February 2006. Anupam Mittal, along with his cousins Anand and Navin Mittal, entered into this agreement with People Interactive and Westbridge Ventures II Investment Holdings, a private equity fund incorporated in Mauritius. Through the SHA, Westbridge Ventures acquired a significant share of People Interactive.

blocks.html: Tensions escalated in 2019, leading Mittal to file a petition before the NCLT in Mumbai on March 3, 2021. This petition, filed under Sections 241 and 242 of the Companies Act, 2013, alleged oppression and mismanagement by Westbridge, with the alleged involvement of Mittal's cousins. However, on March 15, 2021, Westbridge served Mittal with an anti-suit injunction from the Singapore High Court. This injunction restrained Mittal from pursuing his NCLT petition, contending that the disputes were contractual and arbitrable under the terms of the SHA.
blocks.text: Tensions escalated in 2019, leading Mittal to file a petition before the NCLT in Mumbai on March 3, 2021. This petition, filed under Sections 241 and 242 of the Companies Act, 2013, alleged oppression and mismanagement by Westbridge, with the alleged involvement of Mittal's cousins. However, on March 15, 2021, Westbridge served Mittal with an anti-suit injunction from the Singapore High Court. This injunction restrained Mittal from pursuing his NCLT petition, contending that the disputes were contractual and arbitrable under the terms of the SHA.

blocks.html: Mittal swiftly reacted by filing a lawsuit on March 18, 2021, seeking a permanent injunction against the enforcement of the anti-suit injunction. Additionally, he initiated proceedings in the Singapore High Court to challenge the anti-suit injunction's validity. Unfortunately for Mittal, the Singapore High Court upheld the anti-suit injunction in October 2021. Undeterred, Mittal appealed this decision in the Court of Appeal at Singapore in November 2021. It wasn't until January 2023 that the Court of Appeal at Singapore upheld the anti-suit injunction order, leading to Mittal's present interim application in the suit.
blocks.text: Mittal swiftly reacted by filing a lawsuit on March 18, 2021, seeking a permanent injunction against the enforcement of the anti-suit injunction. Additionally, he initiated proceedings in the Singapore High Court to challenge the anti-suit injunction's validity. Unfortunately for Mittal, the Singapore High Court upheld the anti-suit injunction in October 2021. Undeterred, Mittal appealed this decision in the Court of Appeal at Singapore in November 2021. It wasn't until January 2023 that the Court of Appeal at Singapore upheld the anti-suit injunction order, leading to Mittal's present interim application in the suit.
Non-Arbitrability of Claims in India

blocks.html: The Bombay High Court stressed that while the arbitration was set to take place in Singapore, the enforcement of the award would be subject to the Indian Arbitration Act. Importantly, the court highlighted that disputes pertaining to oppression and mismanagement are non-arbitrable under Indian law. The court posed a pertinent question, "What use would be the findings of the arbitral tribunal at Singapore on the question of oppression and mismanagement when the award consisting of such findings can never be enforced in India?"
blocks.text: The Bombay High Court stressed that while the arbitration was set to take place in Singapore, the enforcement of the award would be subject to the Indian Arbitration Act. Importantly, the court highlighted that disputes pertaining to oppression and mismanagement are non-arbitrable under Indian law. The court posed a pertinent question, "What use would be the findings of the arbitral tribunal at Singapore on the question of oppression and mismanagement when the award consisting of such findings can never be enforced in India?"
Upholding the Right to Access Justice

blocks.html: The Bombay High Court firmly upheld the principle that access to justice is a vital component of the rule of law. It also recognized it as a universally acknowledged and constitutionally guaranteed right in India. The court warned against denying a litigant access to justice, particularly when there is no alternative forum available. In Mittal's case, the NCLT was his sole recourse for addressing allegations of oppression and mismanagement. The court ruled that Mittal has the right to seek redressal for his grievances through his petition filed before the NCLT.
blocks.text: The Bombay High Court firmly upheld the principle that access to justice is a vital component of the rule of law. It also recognized it as a universally acknowledged and constitutionally guaranteed right in India. The court warned against denying a litigant access to justice, particularly when there is no alternative forum available. In Mittal's case, the NCLT was his sole recourse for addressing allegations of oppression and mismanagement. The court ruled that Mittal has the right to seek redressal for his grievances through his petition filed before the NCLT.
Court's Decision Favours Mittal

blocks.html: The court found that Anupam Mittal had presented a strong prima facie case for the issuance of a temporary injunction against the enforcement of the anti-suit permanent injunction. It also determined that the balance of convenience weighed in Mittal's favor. Without the injunction, Mittal would be deprived of his only available remedy before the NCLT.
blocks.text: The court found that Anupam Mittal had presented a strong prima facie case for the issuance of a temporary injunction against the enforcement of the anti-suit permanent injunction. It also determined that the balance of convenience weighed in Mittal's favor. Without the injunction, Mittal would be deprived of his only available remedy before the NCLT.

Bombay High Court: Discretionary Power in Section 34(4) of the Act

blocks.html: In a recent landmark ruling, the High Court of Bombay shed light on the interpretation and scope of the expression "where it is appropriate" in sub-section 4 of Section 34, affirming that it confers discretionary power. The case in question, Secretary GOI v. Additional Commissioner, has sparked significant discussion within legal circles due to its implications on the exercise of power by authorities. This article will delve into the background of the case, analyze the ruling of the court, discuss its implications and significance, and finally draw inferences regarding the understanding of discretionary power.
blocks.text: In a recent landmark ruling, the High Court of Bombay shed light on the interpretation and scope of the expression "where it is appropriate" in sub-section 4 of Section 34, affirming that it confers discretionary power. The case in question, Secretary GOI v. Additional Commissioner, has sparked significant discussion within legal circles due to its implications on the exercise of power by authorities. Background of the Case:

blocks.html: The case of Secretary GOI v. Additional Commissioner revolved around the interpretation of sub-section 4 of Section 34 of a specific law. The provision granted power to a particular authority to take certain actions "where it is appropriate." The issue at hand was the determination of whether this expression indicated that the power conferred was discretionary or mandatory.
blocks.text: The case of Secretary GOI v. Additional Commissioner revolved around the interpretation of sub-section 4 of Section 34 of a specific law. The provision granted power to a particular authority to take certain actions "where it is appropriate." The issue at hand was the determination of whether this expression indicated that the power conferred was discretionary or mandatory.

blocks.html: The Additional Commissioner, in this case, had exercised the power conferred by the provision in a manner that was challenged by the Secretary GOI. The dispute arose due to varying interpretations of the expression "where it is appropriate." The matter eventually reached the High Court of Bombay to settle the controversy and provide clarity on the scope of discretion within the provision.

Ruling of the Court:

title: Bombay HC Upholds Arbitration Clause in Invoices | Legal News

blocks.text: News
Bombay High Court Upholds Arbitration Clause in Invoices: A Landmark Ruling

blocks.html: In a significant ruling, the Bombay High Court has affirmed that an arbitration clause embedded within invoices is legally binding, settling a dispute between Sanjiv Manmohan Gupta and Sai Estate Consultants Chembur Pvt. Ltd. The case, adjudicated by Justice Somasekhar Sundaresan, highlights the legal sanctity of arbitration clauses in commercial transactions.
blocks.text: In a significant ruling, the Bombay High Court has affirmed that an arbitration clause embedded within invoices is legally binding, settling a dispute between Sanjiv Manmohan Gupta and Sai Estate Consultants Chembur Pvt. Ltd. The case, adjudicated by Justice Somasekhar Sundaresan, highlights the legal sanctity of arbitration clauses in commercial transactions.
Case Overview

blocks.html: The dispute arose when the petitioner, Sanjiv Manmohan Gupta, provided outdoor advertising services to the respondent, Sai Estate Consultants Chembur Pvt. Ltd. The invoices issued between February 2018 and June 2019 contained an arbitration clause. While partial payments were made, the respondent contested the existence of an arbitration agreement and opposed arbitration proceedings.
blocks.text: The dispute arose when the petitioner, Sanjiv Manmohan Gupta, provided outdoor advertising services to the respondent, Sai Estate Consultants Chembur Pvt. Ltd. The invoices issued between February 2018 and June 2019 contained an arbitration clause. While partial payments were made, the respondent contested the existence of an arbitration agreement and opposed arbitration proceedings.
Financial Stakes and Dispute Timeline

blocks.html: The petitioner sought arbitration after outstanding payments remained unpaid despite the issuance of 20 cheques totaling Rs. 1 crore, of which 10 cheques were honored. Arbitration was invoked on August 22, 2023, but the respondent challenged it, arguing that an arbitration clause within an invoice does not constitute a formal agreement.
blocks.text: The petitioner sought arbitration after outstanding payments remained unpaid despite the issuance of 20 cheques totaling Rs. 1 crore, of which 10 cheques were honored. Arbitration was invoked on August 22, 2023, but the respondent challenged it, arguing that an arbitration clause within an invoice does not constitute a formal agreement.
Court’s Ruling and Key Observations

blocks.html: Justice Sundaresan, while delivering the judgment, noted that “the invoices were accepted and partially paid for without challenging the arbitration clause. The respondent had multiple opportunities to dispute its validity but failed to do so.” The ruling reinforced that even if invoices were marked “received for verification,” the payment and lack of initial objection amounted to tacit acceptance of the arbitration clause.
blocks.text: Justice Sundaresan, while delivering the judgment, noted that “the invoices were accepted and partially paid for without challenging the arbitration clause. The respondent had multiple opportunities to dispute its validity but failed to do so.” The ruling reinforced that even if invoices were marked “received for verification,” the payment and lack of initial objection amounted to tacit acceptance of the arbitration clause.

blocks.html: The court further referenced a similar precedent in Bennett Coleman & Co. Ltd. v. MAD (India) Pvt. Ltd., where it was held that “any document in writing exchanged between parties that provides a record of agreement, without denial from the other side, falls within the ambit of Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.”
blocks.text: The court further referenced a similar precedent in Bennett Coleman & Co. Ltd. v. MAD (India) Pvt. Ltd., where it was held that “any document in writing exchanged between parties that provides a record of agreement, without denial from the other side, falls within the ambit of Section 7 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996.”
Legal Implications

blocks.html: The ruling clarifies that:
blocks.text: The ruling clarifies that:

items.html: Arbitration clauses within invoices are enforceable if the recipient acts upon them.
items.text: Arbitration clauses within invoices are enforceable if the recipient acts upon them.

items.html: Disputes regarding the validity of such clauses should be resolved by the arbitral tribunal, not the High Court under Section 11.
items.text: Disputes regarding the validity of such clauses should be resolved by the arbitral tribunal, not the High Court under Section 11.

items.html: The arbitration process remains valid, even if the matter has previously been litigated under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC).
items.text: The arbitration process remains valid, even if the matter has previously been litigated under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC).

blocks.text: Appointment of Arbitrator

blocks.html: The court appointed Mr. Cyrus Bharucha, a learned advocate, as the sole arbitrator, directing both parties to appear before him to settle the dispute. The arbitration costs will be borne equally by both parties initially, subject to the tribunal’s final decision.
blocks.text: The court appointed Mr. Cyrus Bharucha, a learned advocate, as the sole arbitrator, directing both parties to appear before him to settle the dispute. The arbitration costs will be borne equally by both parties initially, subject to the tribunal’s final decision.

Bombay High Court: Validity of Arbitration Clause in "Sales Terms and Conditions"

blocks.html: In a landmark ruling, the Bombay High Court recently reaffirmed the importance of arbitration clauses and their enforceability in the case of Ingram Micro India v. Mohit Raghuram. The court held that the respondent's acceptance of the arbitration clause, as stated in the "Sales terms and conditions," through the execution of a Know Your Customer (KYC) form, rendered it legally binding. Furthermore, the court emphasized that both parties had acted upon these terms and conditions for a substantial period, thereby establishing the existence of an arbitration agreement. This article provides a comprehensive analysis of the case, delving into the court's rationale and examining the wide-ranging implications of this significant ruling.

This clause specifically stipulated that any disputes arising between the parties would be resolved through arbitration, thereby bypassing traditional litigation methods.

Breaking Barriers: Gender Equality in Indian Arbitration Still a Challenge, Says Chief Justice

blocks.html: In a bid to enhance gender equality within the Indian legal profession, Chief Justice of India (CJI) D Y Chandrachud highlighted a significant gap in representation on international arbitration panels. Speaking at the inaugural session of the United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL) South Asia Conference 2023, Chandrachud brought attention to the low representation of women in the field of international arbitration.
blocks.text: In a bid to enhance gender equality within the Indian legal profession, Chief Justice of India (CJI) D Y Chandrachud highlighted a significant gap in representation on international arbitration panels. Speaking at the inaugural session of the United Nations Commission on International Trade Law (UNCITRAL) South Asia Conference 2023, Chandrachud brought attention to the low representation of women in the field of international arbitration.

blocks.html: The Chief Justice revealed a concerning statistic, stating that "fewer than 10% of Indian arbitrators on international panels were women." This glaring disparity was termed a "diversity paradox" by Chandrachud, especially considering the rise in "regionally diverse panels of arbitrators."
blocks.text: The Chief Justice revealed a concerning statistic, stating that "fewer than 10% of Indian arbitrators on international panels were women." This glaring disparity was termed a "diversity paradox" by Chandrachud, especially considering the rise in "regionally diverse panels of arbitrators."

blocks.html: Addressing the underlying issue, Chandrachud emphasized the role of unconscious bias in favoring male arbitrators and urged for a shift in approach. He referenced a gender diversity report that recommended using "gender-neutral pronouns in our legislation and rules," pointing out the importance of inclusivity in all aspects of dispute resolution.
blocks.text: Addressing the underlying issue, Chandrachud emphasized the role of unconscious bias in favoring male arbitrators and urged for a shift in approach. He referenced a gender diversity report that recommended using "gender-neutral pronouns in our legislation and rules," pointing out the importance of inclusivity in all aspects of dispute resolution.

blocks.html: Chandrachud commented on the growing popularity of arbitration in India, noting that the country has been steadily adopting best practices where arbitrations have become the preferred mode of dispute resolution. Indian courts have actively encouraged the use of Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) mechanisms and remain vigilant against attempts to undermine party autonomy through cleverly crafted contracts.

Cabinet Greenlights eCourts Phase III with INR 7,210 Crore Budget

blocks.html: The Union Cabinet, presided over by Prime Minister Narendra Modi, has given the nod to the third phase of the eCourts Project, sanctioning an impressive financial allocation of INR 7,210 crore over a four-year period. This transformative move, inspired by the philosophy of 'access and inclusion,' is set to revolutionize the Indian judicial landscape.
blocks.text: The Union Cabinet, presided over by Prime Minister Narendra Modi, has given the nod to the third phase of the eCourts Project, sanctioning an impressive financial allocation of INR 7,210 crore over a four-year period. This transformative move, inspired by the philosophy of 'access and inclusion,' is set to revolutionize the Indian judicial landscape.

blocks.html: In a press conference following the Cabinet meeting, Anurag Thakur, the Information and Broadcasting Minister, emphasized that the eCourts Mission Mode Project is at the forefront of enhancing access to justice through technological advancements, aligning perfectly with the Prime Minister's vision of "Sabka Sath, Sabka Vikas, and Sabka Vishwas."
blocks.text: In a press conference following the Cabinet meeting, Anurag Thakur, the Information and Broadcasting Minister, emphasized that the eCourts Mission Mode Project is at the forefront of enhancing access to justice through technological advancements, aligning perfectly with the Prime Minister's vision of "Sabka Sath, Sabka Vikas, and Sabka Vishwas."

blocks.html: The eCourts Project has been under implementation since 2007 as a part of the National eGovernance Plan, with the successful conclusion of Phase II in 2023. Now, Phase III, commencing in 2023, is geared towards promoting 'access and inclusion' across India.
blocks.text: The eCourts Project has been under implementation since 2007 as a part of the National eGovernance Plan, with the successful conclusion of Phase II in 2023. Now, Phase III, commencing in 2023, is geared towards promoting 'access and inclusion' across India.
Key Objectives of eCourts Phase III:

blocks.html: Digital Transformation: Phase III aims to establish a judicial system characterized by digital, online, and paperless courts. This entails the digitization of all court records, including legacy data, and the universalization of e-filing and e-payments through the saturation of court complexes with e-Sewa Kendras.
blocks.text: Digital Transformation: Phase III aims to establish a judicial system characterized by digital, online, and paperless courts. This entails the digitization of all court records, including legacy data, and the universalization of e-filing and e-payments through the saturation of court complexes with e-Sewa Kendras.

blocks.html: Smart Decision-Making: The project intends to introduce intelligent smart systems that facilitate data-driven decision-making for judges and registries, streamlining the scheduling and prioritization of cases.
blocks.text: Smart Decision-Making: The project intends to introduce intelligent smart systems that facilitate data-driven decision-making for judges and registries, streamlining the scheduling and prioritization of cases.

blocks.html: Unified Technology Platform: The primary goal of Phase III is to create a unified technology platform that seamlessly connects the courts, litigants, and other stakeholders, making the judiciary more accessible, reliable, and transparent.
blocks.text: Unified Technology Platform: The primary goal of Phase III is to create a unified technology platform that seamlessly connects the courts, litigants, and other stakeholders, making the judiciary more accessible, reliable, and transparent.
A Collaborative Effort:

blocks.html: The Centrally Sponsored Scheme of eCourts Phase III is a joint endeavor involving the Department of Justice, Ministry of Law & Justice, Government of India, and the eCommittee of the Supreme Court of India. The project will be executed in a decentralized manner through the respective High Courts, with the aim of making the judicial system more accessible, affordable, reliable, predictable, and transparent for all stakeholders.
blocks.text: The Centrally Sponsored Scheme of eCourts Phase III is a joint endeavor involving the Department of Justice, Ministry of Law & Justice, Government of India, and the eCommittee of the Supreme Court of India. The project will be executed in a decentralized manner through the respective High Courts, with the aim of making the judicial system more accessible, affordable, reliable, predictable, and transparent for all stakeholders.

blocks.html: The government has taken steps to bridge the digital divide by ensuring that citizens without access to technology can still access judicial services through eSewa Kendras. Moreover, the digitization of court records aligns with environmental sustainability goals by reducing paper-based filings and minimizing physical document movement.
blocks.text: The government has taken steps to bridge the digital divide by ensuring that citizens without access to technology can still access judicial services through eSewa Kendras. Moreover, the digitization of court records aligns with environmental sustainability goals by reducing paper-based filings and minimizing physical document movement.
Streamlined Court Proceedings:

blocks.html: The expansion of eFiling is expected to streamline the process of submitting documents, reducing human errors as documents undergo automatic checks. This shift towards digitization also allows for the virtual participation of witnesses, judges, and other stakeholders, resulting in cost savings associated with court proceedings, such as travel expenses. Furthermore, court fees, fines, and penalties can be conveniently paid from anywhere at any time.

Furthermore, court fees, fines, and penalties can be conveniently paid from anywhere at any time.
Leveraging Advanced Technologies:

blocks.html: The incorporation of cutting-edge technologies, including AI and its subsets such as Machine Learning (ML), Optical Character Recognition (OCR), and Natural Language Processing (NLP), promises to enhance user experience and create a "smart" ecosystem. Registries will see reduced data entry requirements and minimal file scrutiny, facilitating better decision-making and policy planning. This advanced system will enable smart scheduling and data-driven decisions for judges and registries, enhancing predictability and optimizing the capacity of judges and lawyers.

Virtual Courts Beyond Traffic Violations:

blocks.html: eCourts Phase III seeks to expand the scope of virtual courts beyond adjudicating traffic violations, eliminating the need for litigants or lawyers to physically appear in court. This expansion promises enhanced accuracy and transparency in court proceedings.

Efficient Court Summons Delivery:

blocks.html: The project places significant emphasis on the automated delivery of court summons by expanding the NSTEP (National Serving and Tracking of Electronic Processes), thus substantially reducing trial delays.

A Path to Reducing Pending Cases:

blocks.html: The Union Cabinet's approval of eCourts Phase III with a substantial financial allocation reflects the government's commitment to transforming India's judicial system into a more accessible, efficient, and technologically advanced entity. This endeavour aligns perfectly with the overarching vision of "Sabka Sath, Sabka Vikas, and Sabka Vishwas," as it seeks to ensure justice for all and bridge the digital divide in the pursuit of a more inclusive society.

title: Calcutta HC Directs Composite Arbitration in Rs.12.35 Cr Case
description: Calcutta High Court appoints arbitrator for Rs.12.35 crore HVAC project dispute between Johnson Controls Hitachi and Shapoorji Pallonji.
excerpt: Calcutta High Court appoints arbitrator for Rs.12.35 crore HVAC project dispute between Johnson Controls Hitachi and Shapoorji Pallonji.

blocks.text: News
Calcutta HC Orders Composite Arbitration in Rs. 12.35 Crore HVAC Dispute

blocks.html: In a significant ruling for commercial arbitration in India, the Calcutta High Court, on April 23, 2025, directed a composite arbitration reference in the dispute between Johnson Controls Hitachi Air Conditioning India Ltd. and Shapoorji Pallonji and Company Pvt Ltd., stemming from a Rs.12.35 crore HVAC system project executed across five hospitals in West Bengal.
blocks.text: In a significant ruling for commercial arbitration in India, the Calcutta High Court, on April 23, 2025, directed a composite arbitration reference in the dispute between Johnson Controls Hitachi Air Conditioning India Ltd. and Shapoorji Pallonji and Company Pvt Ltd., stemming from a Rs.12.35 crore HVAC system project executed across five hospitals in West Bengal.

blocks.html: The petitioner, Johnson Controls Hitachi, sought the appointment of an arbitrator to resolve disputes arising from ten work orders issued under a single Letter of Intent (LOI). As stated in the LOI dated November 16, 2015, a composite offer of Rs. 12,35,00,000 — inclusive of material, labor, taxes (excluding VAT and service tax), and incidental charges — was accepted by Shapoorji Pallonji. The work orders followed for projects at Jalpaiguri, Gopiballavpur, Egra, Panskura, and Ghatal.
blocks.text: The petitioner, Johnson Controls Hitachi, sought the appointment of an arbitrator to resolve disputes arising from ten work orders issued under a single Letter of Intent (LOI). As stated in the LOI dated November 16, 2015, a composite offer of Rs. 12,35,00,000 — inclusive of material, labor, taxes (excluding VAT and service tax), and incidental charges — was accepted by Shapoorji Pallonji. The work orders followed for projects at Jalpaiguri, Gopiballavpur, Egra, Panskura, and Ghatal.
Highlighting the unified nature of the transaction, Justice Shampa Sarkar observed,

blocks.html: "The very acceptance of the offer of the petitioner at the consolidated price in respect of the works to be executed at five different locations clearly indicates that the parties, by conduct, had treated the offer of work as a part of the single transaction."
blocks.text: "The very acceptance of the offer of the petitioner at the consolidated price in respect of the works to be executed at five different locations clearly indicates that the parties, by conduct, had treated the offer of work as a part of the single transaction."

blocks.html: The court was convinced by evidence such as emails and payment negotiations that demonstrated consolidated dealings, despite separate work orders being issued for each hospital site. In particular, Johnson Controls argued that the correspondence showed continuous negotiations for composite payments, with partial payments already received.
blocks.text: The court was convinced by evidence such as emails and payment negotiations that demonstrated consolidated dealings, despite separate work orders being issued for each hospital site. In particular, Johnson Controls argued that the correspondence showed continuous negotiations for composite payments, with partial payments already received.

blocks.html: In opposition, Shapoorji Pallonji’s counsel argued that separate arbitrations should occur for each work order, emphasizing that different sites and independent dispute resolution clauses necessitated distinct proceedings. They further contended that some claims were inadmissible.
blocks.text: In opposition, Shapoorji Pallonji’s counsel argued that separate arbitrations should occur for each work order, emphasizing that different sites and independent dispute resolution clauses necessitated distinct proceedings. They further contended that some claims were inadmissible.

blocks.html: However, referring to the Supreme Court’s landmark rulings in Perkins Eastman Architects DPC vs. HSCC (India) Ltd. and Central Organisation for Railway Electrification vs. ECI SPIC SMO MCML (JV), the High Court reiterated the importance of impartial and independent arbitral proceedings. Justice Sarkar cited the apex court’s view:
blocks.text: However, referring to the Supreme Court’s landmark rulings in Perkins Eastman Architects DPC vs. HSCC (India) Ltd. and Central Organisation for Railway Electrification vs. ECI SPIC SMO MCML (JV), the High Court reiterated the importance of impartial and independent arbitral proceedings. Justice Sarkar cited the apex court’s view:

blocks.html: "Equal participation of parties at the stage of the appointment of arbitrators can obviate later challenges to arbitrators."
blocks.text: "Equal participation of parties at the stage of the appointment of arbitrators can obviate later challenges to arbitrators."
Further emphasizing procedural fairness, the judgment noted:

blocks.html: "The principle of equal treatment of parties applies at all stages of arbitral proceedings, including the stage of appointment of arbitrators."
blocks.text: "The principle of equal treatment of parties applies at all stages of arbitral proceedings, including the stage of appointment of arbitrators."

blocks.html: Given these precedents and the unified nature of the transaction, the court appointed Mr. Shashwat Nayak, Advocate, as the sole arbitrator to adjudicate the dispute. His appointment is subject to compliance with Section 12 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, and he will fix his fees in accordance with the prescribed Schedule.
blocks.text: Given these precedents and the unified nature of the transaction, the court appointed Mr. Shashwat Nayak, Advocate, as the sole arbitrator to adjudicate the dispute. His appointment is subject to compliance with Section 12 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, and he will fix his fees in accordance with the prescribed Schedule.

blocks.html: Justice Sarkar concluded that a composite arbitration would be beneficial for both parties, helping avoid fragmented litigation and ensuring consistency in resolving the underlying commercial issues.
blocks.text: Justice Sarkar concluded that a composite arbitration would be beneficial for both parties, helping avoid fragmented litigation and ensuring consistency in resolving the underlying commercial issues.

blocks.html: The court also granted liberty to the respondent’s counsel to file a Vakalatnama within ten days.
blocks.text: The court also granted liberty to the respondent’s counsel to file a Vakalatnama within ten days.

blocks.html: This ruling strengthens India’s commercial arbitration framework by reinforcing party equality, procedural fairness, and the need for independent dispute resolution — key pillars crucial for maintaining investor confidence in Indian infrastructure projects.
blocks.text: This ruling strengthens India’s commercial arbitration framework by reinforcing party equality, procedural fairness, and the need for independent dispute resolution — key pillars crucial for maintaining investor confidence in Indian infrastructure projects.

Calcutta HC Provides Clarity on Arbitrator's Mandate Termination under Section 29A of the Arbitration Act

blocks.html: In a recent landmark ruling, the Calcutta High Court elucidated a crucial legal issue pertaining to the termination of an arbitral tribunal's mandate as outlined in Section 29A of the Arbitration & Conciliation Act, 1996. The ruling highlighted that the mandate of an arbitrator concludes under this section unless an extension is applied for and granted during its tenure, making the arbitrator inadmissible de jure.
blocks.text: In a recent landmark ruling, the Calcutta High Court elucidated a crucial legal issue pertaining to the termination of an arbitral tribunal's mandate as outlined in Section 29A of the Arbitration & Conciliation Act, 1996. The ruling highlighted that the mandate of an arbitrator concludes under this section unless an extension is applied for and granted during its tenure, making the arbitrator inadmissible de jure.
The Legal Dispute in Focus

blocks.html: The Calcutta High Court addressed a pivotal legal question in three arbitration petitions, all questioning the possibility of extending an arbitrator's mandate under Section 29A(4) of the 1996 Act after its termination. The petitioners sought an extension to allow the arbitrators to make and publish awards, while the respondents opposed the plea. The crux of the matter revolved around the timing of the extension application, which had been filed after the expiration of the award-making period specified in Section 29A(1).
blocks.text: The Calcutta High Court addressed a pivotal legal question in three arbitration petitions, all questioning the possibility of extending an arbitrator's mandate under Section 29A(4) of the 1996 Act after its termination. The petitioners sought an extension to allow the arbitrators to make and publish awards, while the respondents opposed the plea. The crux of the matter revolved around the timing of the extension application, which had been filed after the expiration of the award-making period specified in Section 29A(1).
Diverging Legal Arguments

blocks.html: The petitioners argued that an extension request for the arbitrator's mandate could be entertained even after the statutory timeframe outlined in Section 29A(1). They contended that the Court had the authority, under Section 29A(4), to grant an extension before or after the period stipulated in Section 29A(1), provided sufficient cause was substantiated.
blocks.text: The petitioners argued that an extension request for the arbitrator's mandate could be entertained even after the statutory timeframe outlined in Section 29A(1). They contended that the Court had the authority, under Section 29A(4), to grant an extension before or after the period stipulated in Section 29A(1), provided sufficient cause was substantiated.

blocks.html: On the contrary, the respondents relied on the 176th Law Commission Report and Section 29A of the Arbitration Act, stating that an arbitrator's mandate would automatically terminate if an extension application was not filed within the stipulated period. They emphasized that the legislature's intention was to conclude proceedings if an award was not made within the statutory timeframe, as opposed to suspending the mandate.
blocks.text: On the contrary, the respondents relied on the 176th Law Commission Report and Section 29A of the Arbitration Act, stating that an arbitrator's mandate would automatically terminate if an extension application was not filed within the stipulated period. They emphasized that the legislature's intention was to conclude proceedings if an award was not made within the statutory timeframe, as opposed to suspending the mandate.
The Court's Verdict

blocks.html: The Calcutta High Court meticulously examined Section 29 of the Arbitration Act, underscoring that Section 29A(4) and (5) did not offer additional opportunities for extending an arbitrator's mandate beyond the defined extension periods. The insertion of Section 29A in 2016 mandated specific timelines for award-making, altering the prior flexibility in granting extensions.
blocks.text: The Calcutta High Court meticulously examined Section 29 of the Arbitration Act, underscoring that Section 29A(4) and (5) did not offer additional opportunities for extending an arbitrator's mandate beyond the defined extension periods. The insertion of Section 29A in 2016 mandated specific timelines for award-making, altering the prior flexibility in granting extensions.

blocks.html: The Court emphasized that the word "extend" in Section 29-A(4) implied that the mandate of the tribunal must be active at the time of the extension application. Consequently, filing an application after the mandate had terminated was impermissible under Section 29-A. The Court reiterated the importance of parties taking timely steps to extend the arbitral tribunal's mandate within its subsistence.
blocks.text: The Court emphasized that the word "extend" in Section 29-A(4) implied that the mandate of the tribunal must be active at the time of the extension application. Consequently, filing an application after the mandate had terminated was impermissible under Section 29-A. The Court reiterated the importance of parties taking timely steps to extend the arbitral tribunal's mandate within its subsistence.

blocks.html: The ruling clarified that an extension application for an arbitrator's mandate could only be made while the mandate was in effect, and not after its expiration. The statutory language in Section 29-A(4) clearly denoted the termination of the mandate if the application for extension was not made during its continuation.
blocks.text: The ruling clarified that an extension application for an arbitrator's mandate could only be made while the mandate was in effect, and not after its expiration. The statutory language in Section 29-A(4) clearly denoted the termination of the mandate if the application for extension was not made during its continuation.
Significance of the Ruling

blocks.html: This ruling by the Calcutta High Court holds substantial importance in the realm of arbitration and dispute resolution. It sets a clear precedent regarding the extension of an arbitrator's mandate, emphasizing the need for timely actions to expedite the arbitration process and ensure compliance with statutory timelines set by Section 29A of the Arbitration & Conciliation Act, 1996.
blocks.text: This ruling by the Calcutta High Court holds substantial importance in the realm of arbitration and dispute resolution. It sets a clear precedent regarding the extension of an arbitrator's mandate, emphasizing the need for timely actions to expedite the arbitration process and ensure compliance with statutory timelines set by Section 29A of the Arbitration & Conciliation Act, 1996.

blocks.html: Arbitration is a vital tool for resolving disputes outside traditional court proceedings, ensuring confidentiality, efficiency, and specialized expertise. The strict timelines introduced by Section 29A were aimed at expediting the arbitration process and ensuring the timely resolution of disputes. This ruling reiterates the importance of adhering to these timelines and taking proactive steps to extend the mandate when necessary.

The strict timelines introduced by Section 29A were aimed at expediting the arbitration process and ensuring the timely resolution of disputes. This ruling reiterates the importance of adhering to these timelines and taking proactive steps to extend the mandate when necessary.
Legal Interpretation and Implications

blocks.html: The Court's interpretation of Section 29A(4) underscores the necessity of following the prescribed legal procedures in arbitration. It clarifies that an extension application can only be filed while the mandate of the tribunal is in effect. This interpretation aligns with the legislative intent of ensuring a streamlined arbitration process and discourages delays.

It clarifies that an extension application can only be filed while the mandate of the tribunal is in effect. This interpretation aligns with the legislative intent of ensuring a streamlined arbitration process and discourages delays.

blocks.html: The ruling also emphasizes the significance of parties being vigilant and proactive in managing their arbitration proceedings. Parties must initiate extension requests in a timely manner, within the stipulated timelines, to maintain the continuity of the arbitral tribunal's mandate. Failure to do so could result in the termination of the mandate and potential delays in the resolution process.

Parties must initiate extension requests in a timely manner, within the stipulated timelines, to maintain the continuity of the arbitral tribunal's mandate. Failure to do so could result in the termination of the mandate and potential delays in the resolution process.

The ruling underscores the need for parties and arbitrators to adhere to statutory timelines and ensure that extension applications are made within the subsistence of the mandate.

excerpt: In a significant legal development, the Calcutta High Court has granted permission to the State of West Bengal, acting as an award-holder, to withdraw a security deposit of Rs. 9 crore furnished by BBM Enterprises, the award-debtor, in connection with an arbitral award. The withd

Calcutta High Court Allows Award-Holder to Withdraw Rs. 9 Crore Security Amid Arbitral

blocks.html: In a significant legal development, the Calcutta High Court has granted permission to the State of West Bengal, acting as an award-holder, to withdraw a security deposit of Rs. 9 crore furnished by BBM Enterprises, the award-debtor, in connection with an arbitral award. The withdrawal is allowed during the pendency of setting aside proceedings initiated by the award-debtor to challenge the impugned award. The court's decision has shed light on the statutory obligations of an award-holder and the conditions under which the secured amount can be withdrawn.
blocks.text: In a significant legal development, the Calcutta High Court has granted permission to the State of West Bengal, acting as an award-holder, to withdraw a security deposit of Rs. 9 crore furnished by BBM Enterprises, the award-debtor, in connection with an arbitral award. The withdrawal is allowed during the pendency of setting aside proceedings initiated by the award-debtor to challenge the impugned award. The court's decision has shed light on the statutory obligations of an award-holder and the conditions under which the secured amount can be withdrawn.

blocks.html: The recent judgment by a single-bench of Justice Moushumi Bhattacharya has clarified that an award-holder does not require statutory sanction or a separate application during the stay proceedings to withdraw the secured amount. The court ordered the State of West Bengal to provide a bank guarantee of an equivalent amount to the Registrar, Original Side, Calcutta High Court, before proceeding with the withdrawal. This decision was made to ensure that the award-debtor's rights are not prejudiced during the pendency of the setting-aside proceedings.
blocks.text: The recent judgment by a single-bench of Justice Moushumi Bhattacharya has clarified that an award-holder does not require statutory sanction or a separate application during the stay proceedings to withdraw the secured amount. The court ordered the State of West Bengal to provide a bank guarantee of an equivalent amount to the Registrar, Original Side, Calcutta High Court, before proceeding with the withdrawal. This decision was made to ensure that the award-debtor's rights are not prejudiced during the pendency of the setting-aside proceedings.

blocks.html: The application for withdrawing the security amount was filed by the award-holder following changes in circumstances since the court's earlier order in the setting-aside proceedings (AP 746 of 2022). In that order, the court had directed the award-holder to deposit a security amount of Rs. 9 crore in connection with the impugned award, which resulted in the stay of the award. However, with the passage of time, the circumstances changed, leading to the current application for withdrawal.
blocks.text: The application for withdrawing the security amount was filed by the award-holder following changes in circumstances since the court's earlier order in the setting-aside proceedings (AP 746 of 2022). In that order, the court had directed the award-holder to deposit a security amount of Rs. 9 crore in connection with the impugned award, which resulted in the stay of the award. However, with the passage of time, the circumstances changed, leading to the current application for withdrawal.

blocks.html: The court's ruling makes it clear that an award-holder is not obligated to file a separate application for releasing the secured amount during the stay proceedings initiated by the award-debtor. This decision highlights the rights and privileges granted to an award-holder and streamlines the process for securing the awarded sum.
blocks.text: The court's ruling makes it clear that an award-holder is not obligated to file a separate application for releasing the secured amount during the stay proceedings initiated by the award-debtor. This decision highlights the rights and privileges granted to an award-holder and streamlines the process for securing the awarded sum.

blocks.html: Justice Moushumi Bhattacharya emphasized that allowing the award-holder to withdraw the amount upon providing a bank guarantee would not prejudice the rights of the award-debtor during the setting-aside proceedings. This decision aims to strike a balance between the interests of both parties and ensure fairness and justice in the arbitration process.

The bank guarantee is to be obtained from an RBI constituent bank, and the award-holder is given the liberty to take appropriate steps within a reasonable time frame. Simultaneously, the award-debtor is also granted the freedom to proceed with the application for setting aside the award.

title: Calcutta High Court Appoints Arbitrator in Rs. 21 Cr ACC Plant Deal Dispute
description: Calcutta HC steps in as Greenbilt Industries seeks arbitration over a Rs. 21 Cr ACC plant sale dispute with A B Dinesh Concrete Pvt Ltd.
excerpt: Calcutta HC steps in as Greenbilt Industries seeks arbitration over a Rs. 21 Cr ACC plant sale dispute with A B Dinesh Concrete Pvt Ltd.

blocks.text: News
Calcutta High Court Appoints Arbitrator in Rs. 21 Cr ACC Plant Deal Dispute

blocks.html: On March 27, 2025, the Calcutta High Court’s Commercial Division, presided over by Justice Shampa Sarkar, intervened in a high-value commercial dispute between Greenbilt Industries Pvt. Ltd. and A B Dinesh Concrete Pvt. Ltd., ordering the appointment of an arbitrator under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The case revolves around the failed Rs. 21 crore acquisition of an ACC block manufacturing unit in Durg, Chhattisgarh.
blocks.text: On March 27, 2025, the Calcutta High Court’s Commercial Division, presided over by Justice Shampa Sarkar, intervened in a high-value commercial dispute between Greenbilt Industries Pvt. Ltd. and A B Dinesh Concrete Pvt. Ltd., ordering the appointment of an arbitrator under the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The case revolves around the failed Rs. 21 crore acquisition of an ACC block manufacturing unit in Durg, Chhattisgarh.

blocks.html: The dispute stems from an oral and subsequently written agreement for the takeover of the plant, with both sides entering into a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) on February 9, 2022. Greenbilt claimed the respondent had initially agreed to pay Rs.3 crore as advance—of which Rs.2.8 crore was paid—but later failed to fulfill other obligations including the balance advance, remaining sale consideration, and occupational charges.

blocks.html: Greenbilt, represented by advocate Mr. Kumarjit Banerjee, alleged that A B Dinesh Concrete had not only defaulted on payments but also abandoned the plant by the end of 2020 without formal notice. “The MOU was clearly circulated over email and formed a binding arbitration agreement under Section 7(4)(b) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act,” Mr. Banerjee argued.

blocks.html: The case took a legal turn when arbitration proceedings initiated by both parties under the said MOU broke down. Arbitrators were nominated—Sri Bijoy Kumar Tibrewal by the respondent and Sri Prabir Gupta by the petitioner—but the respondent and its nominee failed to appear for the scheduled sitting on April 20, 2023. As per the petitioner, the tribunal’s mandate was thereby terminated.

blocks.html: Quoting from the judgment, Justice Sarkar remarked, “Exchange over email is a valid and accepted form of execution of an arbitration agreement.” She further noted, “Clause 12.3 of the said MOU constituted a valid and binding arbitration clause... Even if the MOU was invalidated for any other reason, the arbitration agreement continued to be a separate and independent agreement.”

title: Calcutta HC Appoints Arbitrator in Rs.15 Cr Govt Contract Dispute
description: The Calcutta High Court appoints an arbitrator in a Rs.15 Cr dispute between ILEAD Foundation and the State of West Bengal over a rural project.
excerpt: The Calcutta High Court appoints an arbitrator in a Rs.15 Cr dispute between ILEAD Foundation and the State of West Bengal over a rural project.

blocks.text: News
Calcutta High Court Appoints Arbitrator in Rs.15 Crore Government Dispute

blocks.html: The Calcutta High Court has stepped in to resolve a financial dispute between ILEAD Foundation and the State of West Bengal concerning a government-funded project under the Ministry of Rural Development. The disagreement, centered around non-payment of dues for the project’s second phase, led the petitioner to seek arbitration after exhausting other legal remedies.
blocks.text: The Calcutta High Court has stepped in to resolve a financial dispute between ILEAD Foundation and the State of West Bengal concerning a government-funded project under the Ministry of Rural Development. The disagreement, centered around non-payment of dues for the project’s second phase, led the petitioner to seek arbitration after exhausting other legal remedies.
Dispute Details:

blocks.html: The ILEAD Foundation, a registered project implementation agency, signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the State of West Bengal on August 30, 2017. The agreement pertained to the execution of a rural development initiative, and the first phase of the project was completed with payment duly processed. However, when the foundation sought payment for the second phase, the State denied the request, alleging non-compliance with the MoU’s terms.
blocks.text: The ILEAD Foundation, a registered project implementation agency, signed a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) with the State of West Bengal on August 30, 2017. The agreement pertained to the execution of a rural development initiative, and the first phase of the project was completed with payment duly processed. However, when the foundation sought payment for the second phase, the State denied the request, alleging non-compliance with the MoU’s terms.

blocks.html: Following this, ILEAD approached the Calcutta High Court in 2022 under Article 226 of the Constitution, but the court dismissed the writ petition in November 2023, stating that the nature of the dispute required an assessment of facts and evidence, which fell outside its jurisdiction. The court, however, clarified that the petitioner could pursue other legal remedies, including arbitration.
blocks.text: Following this, ILEAD approached the Calcutta High Court in 2022 under Article 226 of the Constitution, but the court dismissed the writ petition in November 2023, stating that the nature of the dispute required an assessment of facts and evidence, which fell outside its jurisdiction. The court, however, clarified that the petitioner could pursue other legal remedies, including arbitration.
Legal Arguments and Arbitration Debate

blocks.html: In January 2025, ILEAD invoked Clause 9.1 of the MoU, which provided for arbitration in case of disputes. Despite this, the State of West Bengal refused to participate, leading ILEAD to file an application for the appointment of an independent arbitrator.
blocks.text: In January 2025, ILEAD invoked Clause 9.1 of the MoU, which provided for arbitration in case of disputes. Despite this, the State of West Bengal refused to participate, leading ILEAD to file an application for the appointment of an independent arbitrator.

blocks.html: The State argued that the arbitration clause in the MoU was vague and incomplete, as it did not specify the seat of arbitration or governing law. The government also cited a notification issued in September 2023, which clarified that the term "Arbitrator" in Clause 9.1 should be interpreted as "Adjudicator", thus challenging the validity of the arbitration clause.
blocks.text: The State argued that the arbitration clause in the MoU was vague and incomplete, as it did not specify the seat of arbitration or governing law. The government also cited a notification issued in September 2023, which clarified that the term "Arbitrator" in Clause 9.1 should be interpreted as "Adjudicator", thus challenging the validity of the arbitration clause.

blocks.html: However, Justice Shampa Sarkar, rejecting this argument, ruled that an arbitration agreement does not necessarily require explicit mention of a seat or governing law as long as the intent to arbitrate is clear. She cited previous Supreme Court rulings, including:

items.html: Jagdish Chander vs. Ramesh Chander (2007) – Emphasizing that arbitration clauses must reflect the intent to refer disputes to an independent decision-maker.

items.html: Solaris Chem Tech Industries Ltd vs. Karnataka Urban Water Supply (2023) – Affirming that missing procedural details do not invalidate an arbitration clause.
items.text: Solaris Chem Tech Industries Ltd vs. Karnataka Urban Water Supply (2023) – Affirming that missing procedural details do not invalidate an arbitration clause.

items.html: Perkins Eastman Architects DPC vs. HSCC (India) Ltd (2019) – Holding that unilateral appointment of an arbitrator by an interested party is not legally valid.
items.text: Perkins Eastman Architects DPC vs. HSCC (India) Ltd (2019) – Holding that unilateral appointment of an arbitrator by an interested party is not legally valid.

blocks.text: Appointment of Arbitrator

blocks.html: Considering the failure of the existing dispute resolution mechanism, the court ruled that an independent arbitrator must be appointed. It designated Advocate Sayantan Bose as the sole arbitrator under Section 12 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The arbitrator will determine his own remuneration and oversee the case in compliance with the Act.
blocks.text: Considering the failure of the existing dispute resolution mechanism, the court ruled that an independent arbitrator must be appointed. It designated Advocate Sayantan Bose as the sole arbitrator under Section 12 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The arbitrator will determine his own remuneration and oversee the case in compliance with the Act.
Key Takeaways from the Judgment

items.html: A legally valid arbitration clause does not require explicit mention of governing law or venue as long as both parties agree to arbitration.

title: What Steps Should an Arbitrator Take to Address Limitation Disputes?
description: Discover effective solutions for resolving issues related to limitation through arbitration

blocks.text: News
CHC: Jurisdiction of Chief Justice Under Section 11 Limited to Appointment of Arbitrators

blocks.html: In a significant ruling, the Calcutta High Court bench led by Justice Sabyasachi Bhattacharyya clarified the scope of the Chief Justice's powers under Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The court emphasized that it is beyond the purview of the Chief Justice or their designate when operating under Section 11, to adjudicate on the merits of the case, including any prima facie determination of arguable issues. This responsibility squarely falls within the domain of the appointed arbitrator.
blocks.text: In a significant ruling, the Calcutta High Court bench led by Justice Sabyasachi Bhattacharyya clarified the scope of the Chief Justice's powers under Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. The court emphasized that it is beyond the purview of the Chief Justice or their designate when operating under Section 11, to adjudicate on the merits of the case, including any prima facie determination of arguable issues. This responsibility squarely falls within the domain of the appointed arbitrator.
Key Highlights of the Ruling

blocks.html: Justice Bhattacharyya's bench underscored that all jurisdictional matters, including issues related to limitation, are to be resolved by the arbitrator. The court stated that even if there is some doubt concerning the limitation, it is not within the court's jurisdiction under Section 11 to make a conclusive decision. Instead, such matters must be delegated to the arbitrator.

The court stated that even if there is some doubt concerning the limitation, it is not within the court's jurisdiction under Section 11 to make a conclusive decision. Instead, such matters must be delegated to the arbitrator.

blocks.html: The court's ruling revolves around Section 11, which lays down the procedure for appointing arbitrators and the role of the Chief Justice or their designate in such appointments. The court's decision reaffirmed that the adjudication of jurisdictional issues, including the question of limitation, must be left to the arbitrator.

The court's decision reaffirmed that the adjudication of jurisdictional issues, including the question of limitation, must be left to the arbitrator.
Case Background

blocks.html: The case in question involved B.B.M. Enterprises (the Petitioner) and government authorities. B.B.M. Enterprises completed certain work for the government, confirmed by a completion certificate, with payment up to the second Running Account (R/A) Bill totaling Rs. 1,30,97,481. However, as per Clause 7 of their agreement, the final bill was to be submitted within a month from the completion date. Failing this, the Engineer-in-Charge's certificate of measurement and the total payable amount would be considered final and binding.
blocks.text: The case in question involved B.B.M. Enterprises (the Petitioner) and government authorities. B.B.M. Enterprises completed certain work for the government, confirmed by a completion certificate, with payment up to the second Running Account (R/A) Bill totaling Rs. 1,30,97,481. However, as per Clause 7 of their agreement, the final bill was to be submitted within a month from the completion date. Failing this, the Engineer-in-Charge's certificate of measurement and the total payable amount would be considered final and binding.

blocks.html: While the Respondents paid a substantial portion of the final work value amounting to Rs. 1,59,78,404, a balance of Rs. 24,21,596 and a security deposit refund of Rs. 11,44,205 remained unpaid. The Petitioner referenced the precedent set in M.L. Dalmiya & Co. vs. Union of India (AIR 1963 Cal 277), arguing that the cause of action for payment only arises once the contractor receives a completion certificate. In this case, without such a certificate from the Engineer-in-Charge, the cause of action was not considered to have matured until the payment request was denied.

The Petitioner referenced the precedent set in M.L. Dalmiya & Co. vs. Union of India (AIR 1963 Cal 277), arguing that the cause of action for payment only arises once the contractor receives a completion certificate. In this case, without such a certificate from the Engineer-in-Charge, the cause of action was not considered to have matured until the payment request was denied.
Arbitration Proceedings

blocks.html: The Petitioner issued a notice under Section 21 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, initiating the arbitration process. When the Respondents did not agree to arbitration, the Petitioner approached the High Court under Section 11 for the appointment of an arbitrator.
blocks.text: The Petitioner issued a notice under Section 21 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, initiating the arbitration process. When the Respondents did not agree to arbitration, the Petitioner approached the High Court under Section 11 for the appointment of an arbitrator.

blocks.html: The Respondents contested the application, citing misjoinder and non-joinder of parties. They argued that only the State and the Superintending Engineer, National Highway Circle-I, were relevant parties to the agreement, not the Chief Engineer, P.W.D., or the Executive Engineer, National Highway Division No. I. Furthermore, they pointed out the Petitioner's delay in action, claiming the relevant documents were no longer available due to approximately 20 years of inaction, rendering the claim time-barred.

High Court's Observations

blocks.html: The High Court observed that neither the Petitioner nor the Respondents substantiated the issuance of the completion certificate by the concerned engineer as required by Clause 7. The Petitioner's claim that a portion of the final work value was paid might not necessarily indicate that the final bill was raised and satisfied.

If this perspective were ultimately established, the court pointed out that the limitation bar would be nullified.

Consequently, the court appointed Om Narayan Rai as the sole arbitrator to resolve the disputes between the parties.

Legal Standoff: Calcutta High Court Nixes Arbitration Plea, Declares Time Limitation Paramount

blocks.html: Calcutta High Court recently addressed a plea seeking the appointment of an arbitrator under Section 11(6) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act. In this comprehensive legal analysis, we delve into the intricacies of the case, where a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) signed in 2011 became the epicenter of disputes between the parties. The petitioner faced challenges in receiving the agreed-upon Rs. 1.50 crores and grappled with issues related to the construction of residential flats.
blocks.text: Calcutta High Court recently addressed a plea seeking the appointment of an arbitrator under Section 11(6) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act. In this comprehensive legal analysis, we delve into the intricacies of the case, where a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) signed in 2011 became the epicenter of disputes between the parties. The petitioner faced challenges in receiving the agreed-upon Rs. 1.50 crores and grappled with issues related to the construction of residential flats.
Background of the Dispute

blocks.html: The MoU, executed on 28-03-2011, delineated the disassociation of the involved parties from each other's businesses. The disagreements surfaced when the petitioner did not receive the stipulated amount, leading to conflicts over residential flat construction. The arbitration clause was invoked on 11-03-2014, initiating a series of legal proceedings. The petitioner, dissatisfied with the award, challenged it under Section 34 of the Act, resulting in the award's set aside on 28-09-2022.
blocks.text: The MoU, executed on 28-03-2011, delineated the disassociation of the involved parties from each other's businesses. The disagreements surfaced when the petitioner did not receive the stipulated amount, leading to conflicts over residential flat construction. The arbitration clause was invoked on 11-03-2014, initiating a series of legal proceedings. The petitioner, dissatisfied with the award, challenged it under Section 34 of the Act, resulting in the award's set aside on 28-09-2022.
Section 43(4) Invocation and Respondent's Counter

blocks.html: Relying on Section 43(4) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, the petitioner sought to exclude the period from 11-03-2014 to 28-09-2022. However, the respondent contested the application, asserting the bar of limitation. The respondent emphasized that the notice under Section 21 was issued on 10-07-2023, a staggering 12 years after the alleged right to sue had accrued.
blocks.text: Relying on Section 43(4) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, the petitioner sought to exclude the period from 11-03-2014 to 28-09-2022. However, the respondent contested the application, asserting the bar of limitation. The respondent emphasized that the notice under Section 21 was issued on 10-07-2023, a staggering 12 years after the alleged right to sue had accrued.
Debating Limitation: A Contested Point?

blocks.html: The crux of the matter rested on whether the application was barred by limitation, considering the petitioner's reliance on Section 43(4) of the Act.
blocks.text: The crux of the matter rested on whether the application was barred by limitation, considering the petitioner's reliance on Section 43(4) of the Act.
Court's Legal Analysis

blocks.html: The court referred to Article 137 of the Limitation Act, 1963, setting the limitation period at 3 years from the accrual of the right to apply (27-06-2011 to 26-06-2014). Dismissing the petitioner's notices from 11-03-2014 to 08-05-2023 as irrelevant, the court noted that the arbitration was invoked on 10-07-2023, surpassing the limitation by 4 years and 3 months.
blocks.text: The court referred to Article 137 of the Limitation Act, 1963, setting the limitation period at 3 years from the accrual of the right to apply (27-06-2011 to 26-06-2014). Dismissing the petitioner's notices from 11-03-2014 to 08-05-2023 as irrelevant, the court noted that the arbitration was invoked on 10-07-2023, surpassing the limitation by 4 years and 3 months.

blocks.html: The court emphasized, "...the cause of action arises when the claimant acquires the right to require arbitration. An application for the appointment of an arbitrator under Section 11 of the 1996 Act is governed by Article 137 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act and must be made within 3 years from the day when the right to apply first accrues."
blocks.text: The court emphasized, "...the cause of action arises when the claimant acquires the right to require arbitration. An application for the appointment of an arbitrator under Section 11 of the 1996 Act is governed by Article 137 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act and must be made within 3 years from the day when the right to apply first accrues."
Rejecting Sections 18 and 19

blocks.html: The court rejected the petitioner's reliance on Sections 18 and 19 of the Act, as they presume the subsistence of the limitation period. Section 43(4) was deemed to exclude the period between arbitration commencement and award set aside from the Limitation Act. Despite the exclusion, the application was deemed beyond the limitation period as per Article 137.

Section 43(4) was deemed to exclude the period between arbitration commencement and award set aside from the Limitation Act. Despite the exclusion, the application was deemed beyond the limitation period as per Article 137.

blocks.html: "...the right to apply can only arise when such right is unequivocally denied by the respondent. The claim for arbitration must therefore be raised, without delay, as soon as the cause for arbitration arises similar to a civil action."
blocks.text: "...the right to apply can only arise when such right is unequivocally denied by the respondent. The claim for arbitration must therefore be raised, without delay, as soon as the cause for arbitration arises similar to a civil action."
Clear Ruling on Limitation

blocks.html: The court held that the question of limitation was clear and did not necessitate the arbitrator's determination. It further opined that arbitrators intervene only when the delay is not patently obvious.
blocks.text: The court held that the question of limitation was clear and did not necessitate the arbitrator's determination. It further opined that arbitrators intervene only when the delay is not patently obvious.
Court's Final Decision

blocks.html: In its final decision, the court dismissed the present application as not maintainable, ruling that the petitioner's claims are barred by the laws of limitation. No costs were awarded in this legal standoff.

Calcutta High Court Rules on Arbitration Clause Absence

blocks.html: In a recent legal development, the Calcutta High Court has rendered a significant verdict that sheds light on the relevance of arbitration clauses within contracts. The Court has asserted that the absence of an arbitration clause in the primary agreement holds no sway when that agreement explicitly incorporates another contract containing said arbitration clause. This landmark decision was delivered by Justice Moushumi Bhattacharya, who emphasized the crucial role of Section 7(5) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act (A&C Act) in facilitating the inclusion of arbitration provisions through reference. The case in question presented a scenario where the main agreement unequivocally integrated prior agreements that contained arbitration clauses, thereby aligning squarely with the provisions of Section 7(5).
blocks.text: In a recent legal development, the Calcutta High Court has rendered a significant verdict that sheds light on the relevance of arbitration clauses within contracts. The Court has asserted that the absence of an arbitration clause in the primary agreement holds no sway when that agreement explicitly incorporates another contract containing said arbitration clause. This landmark decision was delivered by Justice Moushumi Bhattacharya, who emphasized the crucial role of Section 7(5) of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act (A&C Act) in facilitating the inclusion of arbitration provisions through reference. The case in question presented a scenario where the main agreement unequivocally integrated prior agreements that contained arbitration clauses, thereby aligning squarely with the provisions of Section 7(5).
Background: The Disputed Contracts

blocks.html: To comprehend the legal intricacies surrounding this case, it is essential to delve into the factual background. The dispute emanated from a notice inviting tender (NIT) issued by the respondent on April 19, 2014. Subsequently, a letter of intent (LOI) was issued on August 21, 2014, and a Work Order followed on November 17, 2014, all in favor of the petitioner.

The dispute emanated from a notice inviting tender (NIT) issued by the respondent on April 19, 2014. Subsequently, a letter of intent (LOI) was issued on August 21, 2014, and a Work Order followed on November 17, 2014, all in favor of the petitioner.

blocks.html: Eventually, on October 15, 2015, the parties executed the primary agreement under scrutiny. Interestingly, both the LOI and the Work Order featured identical arbitration clauses, yet the final contract, executed in October 2015, did not incorporate such a clause. Worth noting is the fact that the agreement dated October 15, 2015, bore the necessary stamp duty, whereas the previous documents did not.
