Supreme Court vs. High Courts: Review Power under Section 11 of the Act

blocks.html: In a recent landmark judgment, the Andhra Pradesh High Court has declared that it lacks the power to conduct a review on the merits of an earlier order passed under Section 11 of the Act. The ruling holds that only the Supreme Court possesses the authority to review its orders passed under Section 11, owing to its inherent power of review under Article 137 of the Indian Constitution. This decision comes after careful consideration of the law and its implications, shedding light on the scope of the High Courts' review powers and the ultimate authority of the Supreme Court in such matters.
blocks.text: In a recent landmark judgment, the Andhra Pradesh High Court has declared that it lacks the power to conduct a review on the merits of an earlier order passed under Section 11 of the Act. The ruling holds that only the Supreme Court possesses the authority to review its orders passed under Section 11, owing to its inherent power of review under Article 137 of the Indian Constitution. This decision comes after careful consideration of the law and its implications, shedding light on the scope of the High Courts' review powers and the ultimate authority of the Supreme Court in such matters.
Background of the Case

blocks.html: The case of Nagireddy Srinivasa v. Chinnari Suryanarayana before the Andhra Pradesh High Court revolved around an application filed under Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. Section 11 deals with the appointment of arbitrators and the role of the court in such appointments. In the matter at hand, an application was made to the High Court seeking the appointment of an arbitrator to resolve a dispute between the parties.
blocks.text: The case of Nagireddy Srinivasa v. Chinnari Suryanarayana before the Andhra Pradesh High Court revolved around an application filed under Section 11 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. Section 11 deals with the appointment of arbitrators and the role of the court in such appointments. In the matter at hand, an application was made to the High Court seeking the appointment of an arbitrator to resolve a dispute between the parties.

blocks.html: During the proceedings, an order was passed by the High Court, appointing an arbitrator to preside over the dispute. Subsequently, a party sought a review of the court's order, challenging the merits of the appointment made under Section 11. This raised the crucial question of whether the High Court could review its own order passed under Section 11 on the merits of the appointment.
blocks.text: During the proceedings, an order was passed by the High Court, appointing an arbitrator to preside over the dispute. Subsequently, a party sought a review of the court's order, challenging the merits of the appointment made under Section 11. This raised the crucial question of whether the High Court could review its own order passed under Section 11 on the merits of the appointment.
Ruling of the Court

blocks.html: The Andhra Pradesh High Court, in its considered judgment, held that it lacks the power to undertake a review on the merits of an order passed under Section 11 of the Act. The Court reasoned that Section 11 grants specific powers to the High Courts to make appointments of arbitrators, but it does not expressly confer the authority to review such appointments.
blocks.text: The Andhra Pradesh High Court, in its considered judgment, held that it lacks the power to undertake a review on the merits of an order passed under Section 11 of the Act. The Court reasoned that Section 11 grants specific powers to the High Courts to make appointments of arbitrators, but it does not expressly confer the authority to review such appointments.

blocks.html: The Court referred to Article 137 of the Indian Constitution, which grants the Supreme Court the inherent power of review. It emphasized that the Supreme Court's review power is not dependent on any specific statutory provision, but rather, it derives from the Constitution itself. The High Court observed that the absence of an analogous provision in the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, indicates that the legislature did not intend to confer review powers on the High Courts in such cases.
blocks.text: The Court referred to Article 137 of the Indian Constitution, which grants the Supreme Court the inherent power of review. It emphasized that the Supreme Court's review power is not dependent on any specific statutory provision, but rather, it derives from the Constitution itself. The High Court observed that the absence of an analogous provision in the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, indicates that the legislature did not intend to confer review powers on the High Courts in such cases.

blocks.html: Furthermore, the Court pointed out that the law relating to the appointment of arbitrators has been clarified by various Supreme Court decisions. These rulings have established that the courts' role in appointing arbitrators under Section 11 is limited to a prima facie examination of the existence of a valid arbitration agreement. Once the court finds the existence of an arbitration agreement, it must refer the parties to arbitration, and it cannot delve into the merits of the appointment. Implications and Significance

title: Tata Capital Loan Dispute Heads to Arbitration
description: Calcutta HC refers Tata Capital’s Rs.3.29 lakh loan dispute to arbitration post-merger; arbitration rights upheld despite non-signatory status.
excerpt: Calcutta HC refers Tata Capital’s Rs.3.29 lakh loan dispute to arbitration post-merger; arbitration rights upheld despite non-signatory status.

blocks.text: News
Tata Capital Loan Dispute Referred to Arbitration After Merger
Tata Capital Loan Default Referred to Arbitration After Merger: Calcutta High Court Rules in Rs. 3.29 Lakh Dispute

blocks.html: In a decisive development, the Calcutta High Court referred a Rs.3.29 lakh loan dispute to arbitration in the matter between Tata Capital Limited and borrower Krishna Kant Tiwari, reinforcing the enforceability of arbitration agreements even post-corporate mergers.
blocks.text: In a decisive development, the Calcutta High Court referred a Rs.3.29 lakh loan dispute to arbitration in the matter between Tata Capital Limited and borrower Krishna Kant Tiwari, reinforcing the enforceability of arbitration agreements even post-corporate mergers.

blocks.html: Presiding over the case AP-COM/1035/2024, Justice Shampa Sarkar observed that Tata Capital Limited, though not an original signatory to the loan agreement, acquired the right to initiate arbitration following its merger with Tata Capital Financial Services Ltd. and Tata Cleantech Capital Ltd., as approved by the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT), Mumbai, effective January 1, 2024.
blocks.text: Presiding over the case AP-COM/1035/2024, Justice Shampa Sarkar observed that Tata Capital Limited, though not an original signatory to the loan agreement, acquired the right to initiate arbitration following its merger with Tata Capital Financial Services Ltd. and Tata Cleantech Capital Ltd., as approved by the National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT), Mumbai, effective January 1, 2024.

blocks.html: Despite the publication of court summons in The Pioneers and Desh Bidesh Haribhumi, no appearance was made by the respondent, prompting the court to proceed ex parte. The petitioner was represented by Advocates Amritam Mandal, Jit Roy, and Aharnish Ghosh.
blocks.text: Despite the publication of court summons in The Pioneers and Desh Bidesh Haribhumi, no appearance was made by the respondent, prompting the court to proceed ex parte. The petitioner was represented by Advocates Amritam Mandal, Jit Roy, and Aharnish Ghosh.
Loan Details and Default

blocks.html: Tata Capital Financial Services Ltd. had disbursed a business loan of Rs. 3,29,000 to the respondent. Following a default in repayment, a loan recall notice was issued on August 12, 2024, but no settlement was made. The petitioner then invoked arbitration on November 7, 2024, in accordance with Clause 9 of the business loan agreement, which stipulated Kolkata as the arbitration seat.
blocks.text: Tata Capital Financial Services Ltd. had disbursed a business loan of Rs. 3,29,000 to the respondent. Following a default in repayment, a loan recall notice was issued on August 12, 2024, but no settlement was made. The petitioner then invoked arbitration on November 7, 2024, in accordance with Clause 9 of the business loan agreement, which stipulated Kolkata as the arbitration seat.

blocks.html: The petitioners argued their case based on the inherited rights from the merged entities and stressed that, under current law, arbitrators can no longer be unilaterally appointed by a party from its own panel—thus, judicial appointment was sought.
blocks.text: The petitioners argued their case based on the inherited rights from the merged entities and stressed that, under current law, arbitrators can no longer be unilaterally appointed by a party from its own panel—thus, judicial appointment was sought.
Legal Reasoning and Precedents

blocks.html: Justice Sarkar emphasized that the petitioner, though a non-signatory, had acquired legal standing due to the merger, citing relevant Supreme Court rulings. In particular, she referenced Ajay Madhusudan Patel v. Jyotrindra S. Patel (2025) 2 SCC 147, where the Apex Court noted:

blocks.html: “The intention of the parties to be bound by an arbitration agreement can be gauged from the circumstances that surround the participation of the non-signatory...”
blocks.text: “The intention of the parties to be bound by an arbitration agreement can be gauged from the circumstances that surround the participation of the non-signatory...”

blocks.html: The Court further elaborated with precedent from Chloro Controls India (P) Ltd. v. Severn Trent Water Purification Inc. (2013) 1 SCC 641, which permitted arbitration between a signatory and a third party under certain legal and factual circumstances.

blocks.html: In another key reference to Cox & Kings Ltd. v. SAP (India) Pvt. Ltd. (2025) 1 SCC 611, the bench highlighted the evolving doctrine of “group of companies,” which enables non-signatories with deep involvement in contract execution to be bound by arbitration clauses.

blocks.html: “The referral court should leave it for the Arbitral Tribunal to decide whether the non-signatory party is indeed a party to the arbitration agreement,” the Apex Court held.

Appointment of Arbitrator

blocks.html: Considering the maintainability of the application, the High Court appointed Mr. Nayan Chand Bihani, Senior Advocate, as the sole arbitrator. He will proceed in accordance with Section 12 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, with liberty to fix his fees as per statutory guidelines.

He will proceed in accordance with Section 12 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, with liberty to fix his fees as per statutory guidelines.

blocks.text: Articles
The Future of Data Protection: Key Insights into the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023

blocks.html: In a transformative step towards fortifying individual privacy rights and reinforcing data security measures, the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA-2023) has emerged as a beacon of hope for data protection advocates and consumers alike. This forward-thinking legislation, meticulously crafted to empower individuals and hold businesses accountable, signifies a significant stride in the realm of data privacy.
blocks.text: In a transformative step towards fortifying individual privacy rights and reinforcing data security measures, the Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDPA-2023) has emerged as a beacon of hope for data protection advocates and consumers alike. This forward-thinking legislation, meticulously crafted to empower individuals and hold businesses accountable, signifies a significant stride in the realm of data privacy.

blocks.html: At the core of the DPDPA-2023 lie its key provisions, each meticulously designed to reshape the landscape of data protection practices. Central to its ethos is the principle of consent and transparency, mandating businesses to obtain explicit consent from individuals before collecting or processing their personal data. Transparency, ensuring individuals are fully aware of how their data is being utilized, stands as a cornerstone of this legislation, fostering trust between businesses and consumers.
blocks.text: At the core of the DPDPA-2023 lie its key provisions, each meticulously designed to reshape the landscape of data protection practices. Central to its ethos is the principle of consent and transparency, mandating businesses to obtain explicit consent from individuals before collecting or processing their personal data. Transparency, ensuring individuals are fully aware of how their data is being utilized, stands as a cornerstone of this legislation, fostering trust between businesses and consumers.

blocks.html: Moreover, the DPDPA-2023 champions the principles of data minimization and purpose limitation, urging organizations to collect only necessary data for specified purposes. By curtailing excessive data collection and usage, this provision mitigates the risk of unauthorized access or misuse, safeguarding individuals' privacy rights.
blocks.text: Moreover, the DPDPA-2023 champions the principles of data minimization and purpose limitation, urging organizations to collect only necessary data for specified purposes. By curtailing excessive data collection and usage, this provision mitigates the risk of unauthorized access or misuse, safeguarding individuals' privacy rights.

blocks.html: In tandem with these principles, the DPDPA-2023 imposes stringent data security and accountability measures on businesses. Robust security protocols are mandated to shield personal data from breaches or unauthorized access, while accountability mechanisms, including regular data protection impact assessments, ensure compliance and prioritize data security as a non-negotiable aspect of operations.
blocks.text: In tandem with these principles, the DPDPA-2023 imposes stringent data security and accountability measures on businesses. Robust security protocols are mandated to shield personal data from breaches or unauthorized access, while accountability mechanisms, including regular data protection impact assessments, ensure compliance and prioritize data security as a non-negotiable aspect of operations.

blocks.html: Crucially, the DPDPA-2023 also addresses the increasingly prevalent issue of cross-border data transfers. With the globalization of data flows, regulations governing such transfers are imperative to ensure data protection remains paramount. The act stipulates specific requirements for businesses engaged in cross-border data transfers, underlining the importance of maintaining data integrity and security irrespective of geographical boundaries.

For ADR organizations, compliance with these regulations is not only a legal obligation but also an ethical imperative. By prioritizing data protection measures, ADR entities can strengthen trust with stakeholders and uphold the integrity of dispute resolution processes.

title: What is the Role of the Arbitration Bar of India?
description: Uncover the realm of arbitration in India with the Arbitration Bar of India
excerpt: Uncover the realm of arbitration in India with the Arbitration Bar of India

blocks.text: News
Announcing a New Era in the Culture of Commercial Arbitration

blocks.html: In India, arbitration plays an important role in global business. The Arbitration and Conciliation Act was introduced in India in 1996 and became operative on 22nd August 1996. The main purpose of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act was to maintain adversarial relationships while engaging in international and domestic business. On the other hand, most people don’t know what arbitration is, or while reading this article most people must be wondering what arbitration is. Arbitration is the process of resolving disputes between the parties in front of the third party, who is neutral. Arbitration was introduced in India to reduce the burden of the courts or to minimise the role of the courts. To avoid the hassle of going to court, people use arbitration to settle disputes quickly and cheaply. The decision made by the arbitrator is final and must be followed by both sides.

Chief Justice of India on Arbitration in London

blocks.html: Chief Justice of India (CJI), DY Chandrachud has travelled to the United Kingdom and delivered a speech on ‘’Arbitration Institutions Should Not Be Controlled By Self-Perpetuating Cliques’’. CJI stated that India should focus on and promote the culture of arbitration. In recent years, the International Arbitration Centre was established with a registered office at Gurugram and other strategically located offices in New Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru and are seeing the continuous stream of arbitration matters. While delivering the speech, Justice DY Chandrachud said establishing only institution for arbitration to resolve disputes is not sufficient, it has to be guaranteed that every voice is heard and valued, these new institutions should not be controlled by a small group of people who keep themselves in power or maintain their control by continuing to include only their own members and not allowing outsiders to join. This group makes sure that it stays in charge by only selecting new members from within its own circle.

In recent years, the International Arbitration Centre was established with a registered office at Gurugram and other strategically located offices in New Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru and are seeing the continuous stream of arbitration matters. While delivering the speech, Justice DY Chandrachud said establishing only institution for arbitration to resolve disputes is not sufficient, it has to be guaranteed that every voice is heard and valued, these new institutions should not be controlled by a small group of people who keep themselves in power or maintain their control by continuing to include only their own members and not allowing outsiders to join. This group makes sure that it stays in charge by only selecting new members from within its own circle.

blocks.html: DY Chandrachud stated that transparency and accountability are the most important ingredients in the Arbitration process, whosoever is involved in this process should have the same information so that it keeps all the things fair which is included in this process. Through fairness, people will trust the process and will accept the decision easily even if they don’t like the decision. This fairness also makes the arbitration process more legit and trustworthy. Transparency also helps to prevent biases, which commonly come when the disputes are being resolved; if biases come in the process of arbitration, both the parties that are included in this process will not be happy with the final results. Through transparency and accountability, the awards are more likely to be enforced by courts, which is really important for making sure the whole process works. Overall, transparency and accountability make arbitration better for everyone involved.

description: Learn about CIBIL scores, their impact on your financial health, and how to improve and check your score. Empower your financial journey with us.

blocks.text: Articles
Know How to Master Your CIBIL Score for Financial Health

blocks.html: In today's fast-paced financial world, maintaining a robust credit history is essential. Your credit history isn't just a record of your debt management and loan repayment—it's a powerful determinant influencing your ability to secure loans, negotiate interest rates, and navigate financial opportunities. Recognizing the significance of this, PDR COURT is launching a dedicated series to demystify the CIBIL score. There remains widespread confusion about credit history, credit score and CIBIL terminologies and their implications, and our goal is to equip you with the clearest, most precise insights available.
blocks.text: In today's fast-paced financial world, maintaining a robust credit history is essential. Your credit history isn't just a record of your debt management and loan repayment—it's a powerful determinant influencing your ability to secure loans, negotiate interest rates, and navigate financial opportunities. Recognizing the significance of this, PDR COURT is launching a dedicated series to demystify the CIBIL score. There remains widespread confusion about credit history, credit score and CIBIL terminologies and their implications, and our goal is to equip you with the clearest, most precise insights available.

blocks.html: Understanding your creditworthiness is pivotal, but where do you begin? Enter the CIBIL score, a pivotal metric that encapsulates your credit health comprehensively. Let’s begin with the basics first:
blocks.text: Understanding your creditworthiness is pivotal, but where do you begin? Enter the CIBIL score, a pivotal metric that encapsulates your credit health comprehensively. Let’s begin with the basics first:
What is a CIBIL Score?

blocks.html: The CIBIL score, provided by TransUnion CIBIL, is a reflection of your credit health. It ranges from 300 to 900, where a higher score indicates a more reliable credit history. This score is derived from your credit report, which includes your borrowing and repayment activities. TransUnion acquired a 92.1% stake in CIBIL to enhance its credit reporting services. They introduced CreditVision to expand the eligible consumer base and drive access to finance for more consumers. Additionally, CIBIL MSME Rank (CMR) was introduced to assess the credit risk ranking of Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs).
blocks.text: The CIBIL score, provided by TransUnion CIBIL, is a reflection of your credit health. It ranges from 300 to 900, where a higher score indicates a more reliable credit history. This score is derived from your credit report, which includes your borrowing and repayment activities. TransUnion acquired a 92.1% stake in CIBIL to enhance its credit reporting services. They introduced CreditVision to expand the eligible consumer base and drive access to finance for more consumers. Additionally, CIBIL MSME Rank (CMR) was introduced to assess the credit risk ranking of Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs).
So, Why is the CIBIL Score Important?

blocks.html: Your CIBIL score is crucial for several reasons. Banks and financial institutions use your CIBIL score to decide whether to approve your loan or credit card application. A high CIBIL score can help you secure loans at lower interest rates and better credit terms and conditions. In some cases, employers and landlords may check your CIBIL score to assess your financial responsibility. Essentially, a good CIBIL score opens up financial opportunities, whereas a poor score can limit them.
blocks.text: Your CIBIL score is crucial for several reasons. Banks and financial institutions use your CIBIL score to decide whether to approve your loan or credit card application. A high CIBIL score can help you secure loans at lower interest rates and better credit terms and conditions. In some cases, employers and landlords may check your CIBIL score to assess your financial responsibility. Essentially, a good CIBIL score opens up financial opportunities, whereas a poor score can limit them.
How is the CIBIL Score Calculated?

blocks.html: The CIBIL score is calculated based on several factors. Repayment history, which accounts for 35% of the score, reflects your track record of making timely payments on loans and credit cards. The credit utilization ratio, contributing 30%, measures how much of your available credit you are using; ideally, this should be kept below 30%. The length of your credit history, making up 15%, is also crucial—the longer, the better. A diverse mix of credit accounts, accounting for 10%, positively impacts your score. Finally, recent credit inquiries, which form 10% of the score, also play a role, with too many new applications potentially lowering your score temporarily.

Finally, recent credit inquiries, which form 10% of the score, also play a role, with too many new applications potentially lowering your score temporarily.
Factors Affecting CIBIL Score

blocks.html: Payment History: Making timely payments on loans, credit cards, and other credit facilities is crucial. Late payments negatively impact the score.

blocks.html: Derogatory Information: Defaults, bankruptcies, or foreclosures can significantly impact the score.

How is the CIBIL Score Used?

blocks.html: Lenders use the CIBIL score to make informed lending decisions. It affects personal loans, home loans, credit cards, and more. A high score can also influence better job opportunities and rental agreements in some cases.
blocks.text: Lenders use the CIBIL score to make informed lending decisions. It affects personal loans, home loans, credit cards, and more. A high score can also influence better job opportunities and rental agreements in some cases.
Common Misconceptions About the CIBIL Score

blocks.html: There are several misconceptions about the CIBIL score. Checking your own score is a soft inquiry and does not affect the score, contrary to popular belief. Closing old accounts can shorten your credit history and negatively impact the score, so it's not always a good strategy.
blocks.text: There are several misconceptions about the CIBIL score. Checking your own score is a soft inquiry and does not affect the score, contrary to popular belief. Closing old accounts can shorten your credit history and negatively impact the score, so it's not always a good strategy.

blocks.html: Additionally, it's not just defaults that affect your score—credit utilization and new credit applications are also significant factors. For instance, if you consistently use more than 30% of your available credit limit or frequently apply for new credit cards or loans within a short period, your CIBIL score can drop, even if you have never defaulted on a payment.

Benefits of a Good CIBIL Score

blocks.html: A high CIBIL score offers numerous benefits. It provides easier access to loans and credit cards, better interest rates, and more favorable loan terms. It also gives you increased negotiating power with lenders, allowing you to secure the best possible financial deals.

Consequences of a Low CIBIL Score

blocks.html: A low CIBIL score can pose several challenges. You may face difficulty in obtaining credit, higher interest rates, and the potential need for guarantors or collateral. These challenges can make it harder to achieve your financial goals and can lead to additional financial stress.

Credit Score vs. CIBIL Score

blocks.html: It's important to note that while the terms "credit score" and "CIBIL score" are often used interchangeably, there is a distinction.

Unstamped Agreements Face Scrutiny: Bombay HC Asserts Authority under Section 11 of A&C Act

blocks.html: In a recent landmark ruling, the High Court of Bombay has solidified its position regarding the treatment of unstamped or insufficiently stamped agreements. The court, while exercising its powers under Section 11 of the A&C Act, emphasized its authority to ascertain and collect unpaid or deficit stamp duty linked to such agreements. This significant step was propelled by a noteworthy judgement from the Delhi High Court in the case of Splendor Landbase v. Aparna Ashram Society.
blocks.text: In a recent landmark ruling, the High Court of Bombay has solidified its position regarding the treatment of unstamped or insufficiently stamped agreements. The court, while exercising its powers under Section 11 of the A&C Act, emphasized its authority to ascertain and collect unpaid or deficit stamp duty linked to such agreements. This significant step was propelled by a noteworthy judgement from the Delhi High Court in the case of Splendor Landbase v. Aparna Ashram Society.

blocks.html: The Bombay High Court stressed that this action could be taken, especially when the quantum of stamp duty is not under serious dispute. The court highlighted its ability to either impound the agreement and send it to the Collector of Stamps, directing the parties to pay the necessary stamp duty and penalties or undertake the task itself through an authorized officer.
blocks.text: The Bombay High Court stressed that this action could be taken, especially when the quantum of stamp duty is not under serious dispute. The court highlighted its ability to either impound the agreement and send it to the Collector of Stamps, directing the parties to pay the necessary stamp duty and penalties or undertake the task itself through an authorized officer.

blocks.html: In the case under consideration, the involved parties had executed an agreement titled 'Employee Non-Disclosure and Non-Solicitation Agreement' on November 15, 2021. However, the agreement lacked the required stamping. Subsequently, a dispute emerged between the parties, prompting the petitioner to invoke the arbitration clause. The petitioner, facing challenges in mutually appointing an arbitrator, filed an application under Section 11 of the A&C Act. The respondent objected to the maintainability of the petition, arguing that the arbitration agreement embedded within the unstamped agreement couldn't be enforced based on a recent Constitution Bench judgement of the Hon’ble Supreme Court.
blocks.text: In the case under consideration, the involved parties had executed an agreement titled 'Employee Non-Disclosure and Non-Solicitation Agreement' on November 15, 2021. However, the agreement lacked the required stamping. Subsequently, a dispute emerged between the parties, prompting the petitioner to invoke the arbitration clause. The petitioner, facing challenges in mutually appointing an arbitrator, filed an application under Section 11 of the A&C Act. The respondent objected to the maintainability of the petition, arguing that the arbitration agreement embedded within the unstamped agreement couldn't be enforced based on a recent Constitution Bench judgement of the Hon’ble Supreme Court.

blocks.html: The petitioner contended that the agreement in question was a non-disclosure and non-solicitation agreement with no monetary value assigned to it. In light of the Maharashtra Stamp Act, the stamp duty payable on such an unstamped agreement could be easily determined. The petitioner proposed a process where the court could grant an opportunity to pay the stamp duty before an officer, who would endorse the payment and transfer it to the Collector of Stamps, allowing the petition under Section 11 of the Arbitration Act to proceed.
blocks.text: The petitioner contended that the agreement in question was a non-disclosure and non-solicitation agreement with no monetary value assigned to it. In light of the Maharashtra Stamp Act, the stamp duty payable on such an unstamped agreement could be easily determined. The petitioner proposed a process where the court could grant an opportunity to pay the stamp duty before an officer, who would endorse the payment and transfer it to the Collector of Stamps, allowing the petition under Section 11 of the Arbitration Act to proceed.

blocks.html: Drawing upon the precedent set by the High Court of Delhi in Splendor Landbase, the petitioner urged the court to adopt a similar approach to expedite the arbitration proceedings while ensuring compliance with the Stamps Act.
blocks.text: Drawing upon the precedent set by the High Court of Delhi in Splendor Landbase, the petitioner urged the court to adopt a similar approach to expedite the arbitration proceedings while ensuring compliance with the Stamps Act.

blocks.html: The court thoroughly analyzed the submissions and upheld its authority to determine and collect the unpaid or deficit stamp duty associated with unstamped or insufficiently stamped agreements. The court referenced the Delhi High Court judgement in Splendor Landbase, reinforcing that this action is justified when the stamp duty amount is not significantly contested. Given the simplicity of the agreement in question, a non-disclosure and non-solicitation agreement with no assigned monetary value, the applicable stamp duty was determined to be Rs. 100 as per Section 5(h)(B) of the Maharashtra Stamps Act.
blocks.text: The court thoroughly analyzed the submissions and upheld its authority to determine and collect the unpaid or deficit stamp duty associated with unstamped or insufficiently stamped agreements. The court referenced the Delhi High Court judgement in Splendor Landbase, reinforcing that this action is justified when the stamp duty amount is not significantly contested. Given the simplicity of the agreement in question, a non-disclosure and non-solicitation agreement with no assigned monetary value, the applicable stamp duty was determined to be Rs. 100 as per Section 5(h)(B) of the Maharashtra Stamps Act.

blocks.html: Consequently, the court directed the Prothonotary and Senior Master to impound the original agreement and collect the determined stamp duty amount, forwarding it to the collector of stamps. Upon payment of the stamp duty, the Prothonotary and Senior Master would endorse the agreement as duly stamped, issue a written certificate, and forward the stipulated amount to the collector of stamps. The court set a timeline of 10 days for completing this entire process.
blocks.text: Consequently, the court directed the Prothonotary and Senior Master to impound the original agreement and collect the determined stamp duty amount, forwarding it to the collector of stamps. Upon payment of the stamp duty, the Prothonotary and Senior Master would endorse the agreement as duly stamped, issue a written certificate, and forward the stipulated amount to the collector of stamps. The court set a timeline of 10 days for completing this entire process.

blocks.html: This recent ruling by the Bombay High Court reiterates the importance of proper stamping and the court's authority to ensure compliance. Unstamped or insufficiently stamped agreements are now subject to thorough scrutiny, emphasizing the significance of adhering to the stipulated stamp duty regulations. The court's proactive approach aligns with the intention of harmonizing the Stamps Act with Section 11(13) of the A&C Act, expediting the arbitration process while upholding legal standards.

title: Unveiling the Potential Smart Contract- Redefining Dispute Resolution with PDR COURT
description: Discover how smart contracts are revolutionizing the way disputes are resolved.
excerpt: Discover how smart contracts are revolutionizing the way disputes are resolved.

blocks.text: Articles
Unveiling the Potential Smart Contract- Redefining Dispute Resolution with PDR COURT

blocks.html: In today's rapidly evolving digital landscape, smart contracts have emerged as a transformative force, revolutionizing traditional contract enforcement mechanisms. These autonomous, self-executing contracts, powered by blockchain technology, offer a seamless and secure way to facilitate transactions across various industries. From finance to supply chain management and healthcare, the adoption of smart contracts is reshaping business operations, optimizing processes, and fostering trust among stakeholders.
blocks.text: In today's rapidly evolving digital landscape, smart contracts have emerged as a transformative force, revolutionizing traditional contract enforcement mechanisms. These autonomous, self-executing contracts, powered by blockchain technology, offer a seamless and secure way to facilitate transactions across various industries. From finance to supply chain management and healthcare, the adoption of smart contracts is reshaping business operations, optimizing processes, and fostering trust among stakeholders.
The Rise of Smart Contracts:

blocks.html: Smart contracts epitomize the fusion of security and transparency in transactional processes. By leveraging blockchain technology, these contracts ensure that agreements are executed precisely as programmed, without the need for intermediaries. The immutable nature of blockchain ledger ensures that transaction history remains transparent and tamper-proof, mitigating the risk of fraud or manipulation. This inherent trustworthiness of smart contracts not only enhances the integrity of transactions but also reduces transaction costs and processing times, resulting in significant operational efficiencies for businesses.
blocks.text: Smart contracts epitomize the fusion of security and transparency in transactional processes. By leveraging blockchain technology, these contracts ensure that agreements are executed precisely as programmed, without the need for intermediaries. The immutable nature of blockchain ledger ensures that transaction history remains transparent and tamper-proof, mitigating the risk of fraud or manipulation. This inherent trustworthiness of smart contracts not only enhances the integrity of transactions but also reduces transaction costs and processing times, resulting in significant operational efficiencies for businesses.

blocks.html: Furthermore, the versatility of smart contracts extends across a multitude of industries, each benefiting from their unique functionalities. In the financial sector, smart contracts are facilitating a myriad of operations, including automated loan agreements, digital asset transfers, and decentralized finance (DeFi) applications. For instance, consider a scenario where a peer-to-peer lending platform utilizes smart contracts to automate the lending process. Borrowers and lenders can enter into loan agreements directly on the platform, with the terms and conditions encoded into smart contracts. Upon meeting predefined conditions, such as repayment deadlines and interest rates, the smart contracts automatically execute the transfer of funds between parties, eliminating the need for traditional intermediaries like banks and reducing the risk of defaults or disputes.

Borrowers and lenders can enter into loan agreements directly on the platform, with the terms and conditions encoded into smart contracts. Upon meeting predefined conditions, such as repayment deadlines and interest rates, the smart contracts automatically execute the transfer of funds between parties, eliminating the need for traditional intermediaries like banks and reducing the risk of defaults or disputes.

blocks.html: Similarly, in supply chain management, smart contracts are revolutionizing processes such as procurement, inventory management, and shipment tracking. By automating key supply chain functions, smart contracts help optimize resource allocation, minimize delays, and enhance traceability throughout the supply chain network. For example, imagine a scenario where a multinational corporation utilizes smart contracts to streamline its procurement process. Suppliers and buyers can enter into smart contracts for the purchase and delivery of raw materials, with payment triggered automatically upon receipt of goods or completion of specified milestones. This not only accelerates the procurement process but also ensures transparency and accountability at every stage of the supply chain.

For example, imagine a scenario where a multinational corporation utilizes smart contracts to streamline its procurement process. Suppliers and buyers can enter into smart contracts for the purchase and delivery of raw materials, with payment triggered automatically upon receipt of goods or completion of specified milestones. This not only accelerates the procurement process but also ensures transparency and accountability at every stage of the supply chain.

blocks.html: In the healthcare industry, smart contracts are ushering in a new era of patient-centric care and data management. These contracts enable secure and interoperable sharing of electronic health records (EHRs) among healthcare providers, ensuring timely access to critical patient information while maintaining patient privacy and data integrity. Moreover, smart contracts facilitate automated billing and insurance claims processing, reducing administrative burdens and ensuring transparent and efficient reimbursement processes. For instance, consider a scenario where a patient undergoes treatment at a hospital and their medical records are stored on a blockchain-based platform.

ABI is Pivotal for India's Strong Arbitration Foothold

blocks.html: In a significant development signaling India's commitment to fostering a conducive environment for dispute resolution, the inaugural ceremony of the Arbitration Bar of India (ABI) marked a pivotal moment in the country's legal landscape. The event, graced by esteemed dignitaries from the legal fraternity including the External Affairs Minister Dr. S. Jaishankar, Minister of Railways, Communications and Electronics and Information Technology Shri Ashwini Vaishnaw, Attorney General for India Mr. R Venkataramani, Solicitor General Mr. Tushar Mehta, and ABI President Mr. Gourab Banerji, underscores the growing recognition of arbitration's pertinence in shaping India's legal ecosystem.

blocks.html: Mr. Gourab Banerji, President of ABI set the tone for the event by expressing gratitude to all attendees and shedding light on ABI's transformative journey. Emphasizing ABI's commitment to promoting gender and regional diversity, Mr. Banerji announced plans to introduce chapters dedicated to women, thereby, fostering inclusivity within the arbitration landscape.

title: What Happens After an Arbitral Award? Finality, Enforcement & Challenges Explained
description: Understand which ADR methods like arbitration or mediation are legally binding and how to ensure enforceability under Indian law.
excerpt: Understand which ADR methods like arbitration or mediation are legally binding and how to ensure enforceability under Indian law.

blocks.text: Articles
What Happens After an Arbitral Award? Finality, Enforcement & Challenges Explained
A Deep Dive into the Post-Award Landscape in Indian Arbitration

blocks.html: The arbitral award is often seen as the conclusive endpoint of a dispute resolution journey. However, in reality, the issuance of an award marks the beginning of a new legal and procedural phase—enforcement. Understanding what follows after an arbitral award is crucial, especially for businesses, financial institutions, and legal professionals who rely on arbitration to resolve commercial disputes.
blocks.text: The arbitral award is often seen as the conclusive endpoint of a dispute resolution journey. However, in reality, the issuance of an award marks the beginning of a new legal and procedural phase—enforcement. Understanding what follows after an arbitral award is crucial, especially for businesses, financial institutions, and legal professionals who rely on arbitration to resolve commercial disputes.

blocks.html: In this article, we explore the legal standing of an arbitral award in India, examine whether it is final, how it can be enforced, and what recourse remains if the losing party refuses to comply or challenges the award.
blocks.text: In this article, we explore the legal standing of an arbitral award in India, examine whether it is final, how it can be enforced, and what recourse remains if the losing party refuses to comply or challenges the award.
Is the Arbitral Award Final and Binding?

blocks.html: Under Section 35 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, an arbitral award is final and binding on the parties and any persons claiming under them. This means that once an award is rendered, it has the same effect as a court decree, subject to any valid legal challenge that may be filed under Section 34 of the Act.
blocks.text: Under Section 35 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996, an arbitral award is final and binding on the parties and any persons claiming under them. This means that once an award is rendered, it has the same effect as a court decree, subject to any valid legal challenge that may be filed under Section 34 of the Act.

blocks.html: However, finality in law often includes contingencies. While the award is deemed final, Indian jurisprudence acknowledges that parties have the right to challenge it on limited grounds, such as fraud, bias, lack of jurisdiction, or violation of public policy. These narrow exceptions ensure that the sanctity of the arbitration process is preserved while allowing judicial oversight.
blocks.text: However, finality in law often includes contingencies. While the award is deemed final, Indian jurisprudence acknowledges that parties have the right to challenge it on limited grounds, such as fraud, bias, lack of jurisdiction, or violation of public policy. These narrow exceptions ensure that the sanctity of the arbitration process is preserved while allowing judicial oversight.
Enforcing the Award: From Paper to Payment

blocks.html: Finality holds little weight without enforceability. This is where Section 36 of the Act comes into play. Once the statutory period for challenging the award under Section 34 has expired—or if such a challenge has been dismissed—the award holder can initiate execution proceedings, just like a civil court decree.
blocks.text: Finality holds little weight without enforceability. This is where Section 36 of the Act comes into play. Once the statutory period for challenging the award under Section 34 has expired—or if such a challenge has been dismissed—the award holder can initiate execution proceedings, just like a civil court decree.

blocks.html: Recent amendments, especially the 2015 and 2019 Amendments, have significantly streamlined the enforcement framework. Before 2015, merely filing a Section 34 petition would result in an automatic stay on enforcement. However, now, a stay must be specifically granted by the court, and that too only upon conditions such as furnishing security. This change was a game-changer for creditors and businesses seeking timely enforcement.
blocks.text: Recent amendments, especially the 2015 and 2019 Amendments, have significantly streamlined the enforcement framework. Before 2015, merely filing a Section 34 petition would result in an automatic stay on enforcement. However, now, a stay must be specifically granted by the court, and that too only upon conditions such as furnishing security. This change was a game-changer for creditors and businesses seeking timely enforcement.

blocks.html: Importantly, enforcement is initiated before a competent civil court with jurisdiction. This typically depends on the seat of arbitration or where the losing party resides or holds assets. The Commercial Courts Act, 2015, has also accelerated these proceedings, ensuring time-bound disposal of execution matters in high-stake cases.
blocks.text: Importantly, enforcement is initiated before a competent civil court with jurisdiction. This typically depends on the seat of arbitration or where the losing party resides or holds assets. The Commercial Courts Act, 2015, has also accelerated these proceedings, ensuring time-bound disposal of execution matters in high-stake cases.
What If the Losing Party Ignores the Award?

blocks.html: A common question in arbitration is whether the respondent can simply choose to ignore the award. The short answer: not without consequences.
blocks.text: A common question in arbitration is whether the respondent can simply choose to ignore the award. The short answer: not without consequences.

blocks.html: Non-compliance can lead to execution proceedings, where courts may attach properties, freeze bank accounts, or even summon directors for willful default. Under Order XXI of the Civil Procedure Code, the award holder can pursue recovery like any other decree-holder in a civil suit.
blocks.text: Non-compliance can lead to execution proceedings, where courts may attach properties, freeze bank accounts, or even summon directors for willful default. Under Order XXI of the Civil Procedure Code, the award holder can pursue recovery like any other decree-holder in a civil suit.

blocks.html: Moreover, deliberate defiance of an arbitral award can also invite penalties under the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971, especially when court directions are flouted post-execution.
blocks.text: Moreover, deliberate defiance of an arbitral award can also invite penalties under the Contempt of Courts Act, 1971, especially when court directions are flouted post-execution.

blocks.html: That said, there are tactical delays that a respondent might employ—ranging from filing a Section 34 petition with a stay application, to engaging in parallel legal proceedings, especially in multi-jurisdictional disputes. This is why institutional and tech-backed platforms play a critical role in tracking post-award progress and reducing procedural hurdles.
blocks.text: That said, there are tactical delays that a respondent might employ—ranging from filing a Section 34 petition with a stay application, to engaging in parallel legal proceedings, especially in multi-jurisdictional disputes. This is why institutional and tech-backed platforms play a critical role in tracking post-award progress and reducing procedural hurdles.
Grounds for Challenge: Section 34 Explained

blocks.html: A losing party may attempt to set aside the award under Section 34, but this provision is not an appeal mechanism. The Supreme Court has reiterated time and again that courts cannot re-appreciate evidence or sit in judgment over the arbitrator’s reasoning.
blocks.text: A losing party may attempt to set aside the award under Section 34, but this provision is not an appeal mechanism. The Supreme Court has reiterated time and again that courts cannot re-appreciate evidence or sit in judgment over the arbitrator’s reasoning.

blocks.html: Permissible grounds include:
blocks.text: Permissible grounds include:

items.html: Incapacity of a party or invalid arbitration agreement
items.text: Incapacity of a party or invalid arbitration agreement

items.html: Violation of principles of natural justice (e.g., one party not heard)
items.text: Violation of principles of natural justice (e.g., one party not heard)

items.html: Excess of jurisdiction by arbitrators
items.text: Excess of jurisdiction by arbitrators

items.html: Award in conflict with the public policy of India
items.text: Award in conflict with the public policy of India

items.html: Fraud or corruption during proceedings
items.text: Fraud or corruption during proceedings

blocks.html: Even here, courts apply a high threshold of proof. The judiciary has repeatedly cautioned against misuse of Section 34 to delay or derail enforcement, thereby reinforcing the pro-arbitration stance of Indian courts post-2015.
blocks.text: Even here, courts apply a high threshold of proof. The judiciary has repeatedly cautioned against misuse of Section 34 to delay or derail enforcement, thereby reinforcing the pro-arbitration stance of Indian courts post-2015.
Practical Scenarios: What to Expect

blocks.html: In real-world scenarios, especially in debt recovery and commercial disputes, the post-award phase unfolds in layered stages:
blocks.text: In real-world scenarios, especially in debt recovery and commercial disputes, the post-award phase unfolds in layered stages:

items.html: Award Delivery: Sent to parties via registered post or digitally.
items.text: Award Delivery: Sent to parties via registered post or digitally.

items.html: Cooling-Off Period: A statutory three-month period (extendable by 30 days) to challenge under Section 34.
items.text: Cooling-Off Period: A statutory three-month period (extendable by 30 days) to challenge under Section 34.

items.html: Execution Filing: If uncontested, the winner moves to enforcement under Section 36.
items.text: Execution Filing: If uncontested, the winner moves to enforcement under Section 36.

items.html: Stay Proceedings: If contested, the court may impose a conditional or absolute stay.
items.text: Stay Proceedings: If contested, the court may impose a conditional or absolute stay.

items.html: Asset Seizure or Settlement: Based on court orders, recovery is pursued or settlement negotiated.
items.text: Asset Seizure or Settlement: Based on court orders, recovery is pursued or settlement negotiated.

blocks.html: For lenders like banks and NBFCs, this clarity in enforcement is vital, especially in high-volume or non-performing asset (NPA) cases. Time-bound enforcement ensures better recovery rates and improves investor confidence in ADR mechanisms.
blocks.text: For lenders like banks and NBFCs, this clarity in enforcement is vital, especially in high-volume or non-performing asset (NPA) cases. Time-bound enforcement ensures better recovery rates and improves investor confidence in ADR mechanisms.
How Neutral ADR Platforms Like PDR COURT Help

blocks.html: PDR COURT, a neutral technology-driven ADR platform, facilitates end-to-end resolution while remaining legally compliant and unbiased. While it does not operate as an arbitral institution, it provides critical support across the post-award lifecycle—digital delivery of awards, follow-ups, guidance on enforcement procedures, and partner-led legal escalation if needed.

While it does not operate as an arbitral institution, it provides critical support across the post-award lifecycle—digital delivery of awards, follow-ups, guidance on enforcement procedures, and partner-led legal escalation if needed.

blocks.html: Its platform-based model especially aids NBFCs, fintechs, and MSMEs who need cost-effective, enforceable resolutions. By reducing dependency on conventional legal channels, PDR COURT helps parties move swiftly from award to recovery.
blocks.text: Its platform-based model especially aids NBFCs, fintechs, and MSMEs who need cost-effective, enforceable resolutions. By reducing dependency on conventional legal channels, PDR COURT helps parties move swiftly from award to recovery.

blocks.html: Understanding what follows after an arbitral award is as important as knowing how to invoke arbitration in the first place. With the evolving legal landscape and a pro-enforcement stance from Indian courts, awards now have teeth—provided parties act swiftly and strategically. For institutions and businesses alike, this post-award clarity enhances the credibility and reliability of arbitration as a powerful tool for dispute resolution.

title: Why Did Yuvraj Singh End up in an Arbitration with a Real Estate Agency?
description:

case law arbitration and conciliation act 1996
excerpt:
Yuvraj Singh arbitration cases on real estate agency, Arbitration cases by Yuvraj Singh ,Cricketer Yuvraj Singh arbitration case on real estate agency, yuvraj singh arbitration case,Yuvraj cases under arbitration,

blocks.text: News
Power of Arbitration Seen in Yuvraj Singh's Legal Battle

blocks.html: In a high-profile legal move, cricketer Yuvraj Singh has invoked arbitration against a developer, M/s Brilliant Etoile Private Limited, citing alleged violations of his privacy rights and failure to meet the agreed timeline for the delivery of possession of an apartment in a real estate project in the national capital. This action has brought attention to the ongoing issues faced by public figures in their business dealings, particularly in the real estate sector.
blocks.text: In a high-profile legal move, cricketer Yuvraj Singh has invoked arbitration against a developer, M/s Brilliant Etoile Private Limited, citing alleged violations of his privacy rights and failure to meet the agreed timeline for the delivery of possession of an apartment in a real estate project in the national capital. This action has brought attention to the ongoing issues faced by public figures in their business dealings, particularly in the real estate sector.

blocks.html: Yuvraj Singh has issued two arbitration notices to the developer. The first notice addresses the alleged misuse of his brand value and violations of the terms of the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) dated November 24, 2020. According to the MoU, Yuvraj Singh was to promote and endorse the real estate project. However, despite the MoU expiring on November 23 of the previous year, the developer has allegedly continued to use Singh’s services commercially. This includes the unauthorized use of his photographs on billboards, the project site, social media posts, and articles. Yuvraj Singh argues that this continued use violates his Copyright, Personality Rights, and Right to Publicity, which are protected under Intellectual Property Rights laws.
blocks.text: Yuvraj Singh has issued two arbitration notices to the developer. The first notice addresses the alleged misuse of his brand value and violations of the terms of the Memorandum of Understanding (MoU) dated November 24, 2020. According to the MoU, Yuvraj Singh was to promote and endorse the real estate project. However, despite the MoU expiring on November 23 of the previous year, the developer has allegedly continued to use Singh’s services commercially. This includes the unauthorized use of his photographs on billboards, the project site, social media posts, and articles. Yuvraj Singh argues that this continued use violates his Copyright, Personality Rights, and Right to Publicity, which are protected under Intellectual Property Rights laws.

blocks.html: The second notice pertains to the delivery of the apartment promised to Yuvraj Singh. The cricketer alleges that the developer has failed to adhere to the agreed timeline and schedule for delivery, as stipulated in the Agreement to Sale. Moreover, Yuvraj Singh claims that the developer compromised on the quality of materials and downgraded the fittings, furnishings, lighting, and finishing of the apartment. He further alleges that the developer charged an escalated price beyond the actual market value and failed to maintain promised standards, resulting in poor and unhygienic surroundings, thus breaching the terms of the Agreement to Sale.
blocks.text: The second notice pertains to the delivery of the apartment promised to Yuvraj Singh. The cricketer alleges that the developer has failed to adhere to the agreed timeline and schedule for delivery, as stipulated in the Agreement to Sale. Moreover, Yuvraj Singh claims that the developer compromised on the quality of materials and downgraded the fittings, furnishings, lighting, and finishing of the apartment. He further alleges that the developer charged an escalated price beyond the actual market value and failed to maintain promised standards, resulting in poor and unhygienic surroundings, thus breaching the terms of the Agreement to Sale.
